target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 must resolve ISS-001 (label poisoning) and ISS-002 (mixed model versions). For ISS-001, I will specify a CUSUM control chart on per-operator false positive rate with a k=0.5, h=5 threshold triggering automatic label hold and MLOps team alert. For ISS-002, I will define weld_cell_id deployment group tags in the IoT Edge deployment manifest so canary promotion is cell-atomic. ISS-003 (latency benchmark) I will address with a hardware benchmark protocol specification.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified CUSUM control chart (k=0.5, h=5) on per-operator false positive acceptance rate; when cumulative sum exceeds h=5 the operator label queue is automatically suspended and MLOps admin notified via PagerDuty; labels are held for secondary review before ingestion into training dataset
fix_report.expected_outcome: Label poisoning attacks detectable within 40-50 labeling events per operator; automatic hold prevents contaminated labels entering training pipeline

fix_report.action_taken: Defined weld_cell_id tag on each IoT Edge device twin; canary deployment manager groups devices by weld_cell_id and applies model update atomically within each cell group before advancing to next group; rollout pauses if any node in a cell fails health check post-update
fix_report.expected_outcome: All robots in a weld cell always run identical model version; quality engineers see consistent rework flag rates throughout rollout

target_output.title: Weld Acoustic Anomaly Detection System - Body-in-White Line
target_output.executive_summary: A high-scale AI system deploying 1D-CNN acoustic emission analysis on Azure IoT Edge gateway nodes across 60+ body-in-white welding robots, delivering rework flags to the plant MES within 150 ms of weld completion. Online learning from quality engineer labels is protected by CUSUM operator monitoring; canary model rollouts are enforced at weld-cell granularity. ClickHouse stores all detection events with full IATF 16949 traceability fields.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture: OT edge tier (Azure IoT Edge gateway nodes, 1 per 16-robot cell zone) runs the Rust Axum EdgeInferenceService extracting 18 acoustic features per weld event (RMS, peak frequency, spectral centroid, kurtosis, crest factor) and scoring a quantized 1D-CNN model within a 150ms wall-clock budget (feature extraction 45ms, inference 65ms, flag emission 40ms). IT backend tier on AKS hosts FastAPI MLOpsAPI for model lifecycle management, React quality engineer dashboard, and ClickHouse connector. MLOps tier on AKS GPU node pool runs weekly challenger training jobs. Edge-to-backend communication over mTLS gRPC; edge nodes have no direct internet access and connect to AKS via Azure Private Link through the plant DMZ.
target_output.technology_stack: Edge inference: Rust 1.77 with Axum, tract inference runtime for quantized ONNX models, custom acoustic DSP crate. MLOps backend: Python 3.12 with FastAPI, PyTorch for training, ONNX export pipeline. Frontend: React 18 with TypeScript, Recharts for real-time anomaly charts. Data: ClickHouse 24 on AKS with MergeTree + TTL policies, Redis 7 for edge deduplication. Infrastructure: Azure IoT Edge, AKS, Azure Container Registry, Azure Key Vault, Azure IoT Hub for device management.
target_output.functional_feature_map: EdgeInferenceService: acoustic feature extraction from raw PCM stream, 1D-CNN ONNX inference, rework flag emission to MES via gRPC with weld_joint_id + model_version + confidence_score payload. QualityEngineerDashboard: real-time rework flag queue with LLM-generated anomaly explanations, label submission (confirm/reject), operator-level CUSUM chart visualization. MLOpsAPI: model version management, canary deployment initiation with cell-group targeting, training job trigger, golden test set evaluation results. ThresholdConfigUI: per-robot and per-anomaly-type confidence threshold adjustment with audit trail.
target_output.system_components: IoT Edge Gateway Nodes (4x, 1 per body shop zone): EdgeInferenceService (Rust module), AcousticCollectorModule (reads USB DAQ from acoustic sensor array), RedisEdge (deduplication). AKS System Pool: MLOpsAPI (FastAPI), React Dashboard (Nginx), gRPC-to-MES Bridge (Go sidecar). AKS MLOps Pool (GPU): WeeklyTrainingJob (PyTorch, triggered by Argo Workflows). AKS Data Pool: ClickHouse StatefulSet, Redis StatefulSet. Azure IoT Hub: device twin management and OTA module updates. Azure Key Vault: model signing certificates, edge device CA.
target_output.workflows: Weld Event Inference: AcousticCollectorModule receives PCM frame at weld start signal from robot PLC digital I/O; EdgeInferenceService extracts 18 features using custom Rust DSP crate, runs quantized 1D-CNN via tract runtime, computes anomaly_type and confidence_score; if confidence exceeds robot-specific threshold, emits rework_flag gRPC call to MES Bridge; entire pipeline completes within 150ms of weld completion trigger. Label Feedback and CUSUM Monitoring: quality engineer reviews rework flag in dashboard with LLM-generated explanation; submits confirm or reject label; label ingestion service computes running CUSUM(k=0.5, h=5) on per-operator false positive acceptance rate; if cumulative sum S_n > 5, operator queue is suspended, pending labels held, PagerDuty P2 alert sent to MLOps admin for secondary review before labels enter training store.
target_output.data_model: welding_robots (id UUID PK, cell_id, robot_type ENUM spot/arc/laser, acoustic_sensor_id, weld_cell_id for deployment grouping, calibration_date, active BOOL). weld_joints (id UUID PK, robot_id FK, joint_type, nominal_energy_joules, drawing_ref, target_spec_id). acoustic_samples (id UUID PK, weld_joint_id FK, sample_rate_hz INT, duration_ms SMALLINT, feature_vector_json JSONB storing 18 extracted features, raw_pcm_s3_key, captured_at DATETIME). anomaly_detections (id UUID PK, weld_joint_id FK, model_version_id FK, anomaly_type ENUM cold_weld/spatter/arc_instability/nominal, confidence_score FLOAT32, inference_latency_ms SMALLINT, detected_at DATETIME) stored in ClickHouse MergeTree partitioned by toYYYYMM(detected_at). rework_flags (id UUID PK, anomaly_detection_id FK, mes_work_order_ref, status ENUM pending/confirmed/rejected, reviewed_by, reviewed_at). model_deployments (id UUID PK, model_version VARCHAR, checksum_sha256, weld_cell_ids_targeted ARRAY, deployed_node_count, promoted_at, promoted_by). operator_cusum_stats (operator_id, label_date DATE, fp_count INT, cusum_s_n FLOAT, suspended BOOL).
target_output.api_design: MLOpsAPI (FastAPI, /api/v1): GET /models — list model versions with F1 scores and deployment status. POST /models/{version}/deploy — initiate cell-group canary with body {weld_cell_ids: [...], canary_pct: 0.25}. GET /flags?status=pending&robot_id= — quality engineer flag queue. PATCH /flags/{id}/label — submit confirm/reject label with operator JWT claim. GET /operators/{id}/cusum — retrieve CUSUM time series for monitoring dashboard. POST /operators/{id}/unsuspend — MLOps admin action after secondary label review. GET /analytics/anomaly-rate?cell_id=&from=&to= — per-cell anomaly rate trend. MES Bridge gRPC: ReworkFlagService.EmitFlag(ReworkFlagRequest) returns (Ack) with retry semantics.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure AD workload identity for all AKS pods; IoT Edge modules authenticate via X.509 device certificates issued by plant CA (rotated every 90 days via SCEP protocol). mTLS enforced on all edge-to-AKS gRPC connections via Istio sidecar. Model artifacts signed with RSA-4096 key in Azure Key Vault; EdgeInferenceService verifies signature before loading model into tract runtime. IATF 16949 traceability: anomaly_detections table includes model_version_id, robot_id, weld_joint_id, and confidence_score on every row; append-only, no DELETE or UPDATE permitted. Label poisoning control: CUSUM(k=0.5, h=5) on per-operator false positive acceptance rate; automatic suspension and P2 PagerDuty alert when S_n > 5. Deployment audit: every model promotion event logged with actor Azure AD OID, target weld_cell_ids, and SHA-256 checksum.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure DevOps Pipeline stages: (1) Rust clippy + cargo test on EdgeInferenceService, (2) Python pytest with mock acoustic fixture covering inference path and CUSUM calculation, (3) Docker build and ACR push for all services, (4) Integration test: spin up 2-node IoT Edge simulator, inject 100 synthetic weld events, assert rework flags emitted within 150ms and written to ClickHouse, (5) Staging deploy to 5-robot pilot cell with 30-min soak and latency p95 check, (6) Production deploy requires MLOps team approval. Cell-atomic canary: IoT Edge deployment manifest assigns weld_cell_id tag to each device; CellGroupDeploymentController (custom Go operator on AKS) advances rollout cell-by-cell, verifying all nodes in a cell pass health probe before moving to next cell. Rollback: twin desired property reverts to previous model_version_id, propagated within 60 seconds to all cell members.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics from each IoT Edge gateway node: weld_inference_latency_ms histogram (SLA alert: p95 > 130ms), weld_events_total counter, rework_flags_emitted_total, edge_node_heartbeat_timestamp. Grafana dashboard per body shop zone with per-robot anomaly rate, false positive rate trend, and CUSUM S_n chart per operator. ClickHouse system.query_log for analytics query performance. PagerDuty P1: edge node heartbeat loss > 2 min or inference p99 > 148ms. PagerDuty P2: CUSUM suspension triggered, model deployment failure, training job F1 below 0.92 gate.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Primary cost: AKS GPU node pool (Standard_NC6s_v3) for weekly training runs — estimated 4 hours/week at ~$3.00/hr = ~$52/month. ClickHouse AKS node pool (Standard_D8s_v3): ~$280/month. IoT Edge gateway hardware is plant-owned CAPEX. Total Azure OpEx estimated at ~$450/month for 60-robot deployment. Scaling: additional weld cells require only new IoT Edge gateway provisioning; AKS backend auto-scales on CPU 60% target up to 6 replicas per service.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Pilot (10 weeks): Deploy EdgeInferenceService and MLOpsAPI on 20-robot pilot cell; integrate rework flag delivery to MES; quality engineer dashboard with basic flag review; establish CUSUM monitoring baseline. Phase 2 Full Rollout (6 weeks): Extend to all 60+ robots with cell-atomic canary deployment; implement LLM anomaly explanation integration; full IATF 16949 audit trail validation. Phase 3 Online Learning (4 weeks): Weekly challenger training pipeline on AKS GPU pool; CUSUM-gated label ingestion; A/B evaluation framework for model promotion decisions; cross-cell model performance analytics.
target_output.development_guidelines: Rust EdgeInferenceService: all acoustic DSP functions have unit tests with golden PCM fixtures asserting feature values to 4 decimal places. No unwrap() in inference path — all errors return typed Result and emit Prometheus error counter. Python MLOps: pytest fixtures use recorded weld event payloads from pilot, not synthetic data. CUSUM logic covered by property-based tests with Hypothesis. API contracts versioned via FastAPI OpenAPI export committed to repo. IoT Edge module manifests managed as Helm-equivalent YAML in /infrastructure/iotedge with GitOps reconciliation via Flux.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: acoustic sensor firmware differences across robot generations produce feature distribution shift that degrades model accuracy. Mitigation: per-robot acoustic_sensor_id tracked in welding_robots table; training dataset stratified by sensor type. Risk: CUSUM threshold (k=0.5, h=5) too sensitive during model transition periods when legitimate false positive rates temporarily increase. Mitigation: CUSUM reset allowed by MLOps admin with audit log entry after model promotion events. Tradeoff: quantized ONNX model via tract reduces inference memory footprint at cost of ~1% F1 vs full precision; acceptable given 150ms constraint. Tradeoff: weekly retraining cadence vs daily — weekly chosen to accumulate sufficient label volume for statistically significant accuracy delta.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-001 resolved: CUSUM(k=0.5, h=5) control chart with automatic operator suspension adopted over simpler threshold because it is robust to gradual drift not just sudden spikes. ISS-002 resolved: weld_cell_id device twin tag with CellGroupDeploymentController Go operator provides cell-atomic rollout without requiring IoT Hub deployment group feature which lacks cell-level granularity. ISS-003 partially resolved: hardware benchmark protocol defined (100-weld replay on target gateway hardware in staging); benchmark gate added to Phase 1 pilot acceptance criteria.

metadata.notes: Round 3 weld acoustic AI: resolves label poisoning CUSUM spec and cell-atomic canary deployment for body-in-white line.

project_goal.value: CLI tool for manufacturing engineers to analyze OEE data exports from multiple SCADA systems, classify downtime and micro-stop events by loss category, compute annualized cost impact per machine using configurable hourly rates, and generate prioritized top-10 opportunity reports in CSV and PDF
project_goal.rationale: Replaces error-prone manual spreadsheet analysis for TPM improvement prioritization

target_users.value: Manufacturing and maintenance engineers running analysis on local Windows workstations
target_users.rationale: Single-user CLI; no multi-user or server requirements

project_class.rationale: Local analysis tool with file I/O and no hosted service

capabilities.rationale: Data ingestion, DuckDB query engine, batch report generation

complexity_level.rationale: Single-process CLI with file I/O and DuckDB; no distributed components

risk_level.rationale: Read-only analysis of internal SCADA exports; no write-back to production systems

data_sensitivity.rationale: OEE data is internal operational data; not customer-facing or personally identifiable

external_exposure.rationale: CLI executes entirely on local workstation with no network endpoints

access_model.value: OS-level file permissions; no application authentication required
access_model.rationale: Tool reads local files; access governed by OS permissions

feature_scope.value: Multi-format SCADA export parsing (CSV, Excel), OEE decomposition into availability/performance/quality loss buckets, fault-code classification via configurable YAML rules, annualized cost impact per machine, top-10 ranked opportunity report, CSV and PDF output
feature_scope.rationale: Full approved feature set

mvp_scope.value: CSV SCADA parser, loss classification, cost calculation, CSV report output; PDF deferred to v1.1
mvp_scope.rationale: CSV sufficient for initial TPM team adoption

security_baseline.value: No hardcoded credentials; no network calls; path traversal check on all input paths; reports labeled INTERNAL CONFIDENTIAL
security_baseline.rationale: Minimal security surface for local analysis tool

frontend_stack.value: None (CLI only; Rich library for stdout formatting)
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI tool with stdout/file output; no browser UI applicable

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Typer for CLI, DuckDB for in-process analytics
backend_stack.rationale: Typer for type-validated CLI; DuckDB reads CSV natively and executes SQL aggregations without a server

data_platform.value: DuckDB in-process (file-backed mode optional for debugging); Parquet intermediate for multi-file joins
data_platform.rationale: DuckDB handles 500K-row SCADA datasets with sub-10s SQL aggregations on workstation hardware

hosting_target.value: Local workstation installation via pip from internal PyPI mirror
hosting_target.rationale: Engineers install and run locally; no server deployment

privacy_retention_policy.value: Tool does not persist data; generated reports retained per local filesystem policy; no cloud upload
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No data retention policy applicable to local analysis tool

future_scope.value: Excel SCADA format support, PDF reports via WeasyPrint, interactive TUI threshold tuning, SAP PM work order suggestion export
future_scope.rationale: v1.1 and v2.0 roadmap items

constraints.value: Must run on Windows 10/11; 12-month SCADA analysis must complete within 60 seconds; fully offline capable
constraints.rationale: Plant workstations are Windows-only; engineers expect near-instant results without internet

observability_baseline.value: --verbose flag for per-step progress to stdout; --log-file for structured JSON log to disk
observability_baseline.rationale: CLI tools use stdout/file logging; no external monitoring infrastructure

execution_preference.value: Single-developer iterative releases with GitHub Actions CI for lint, type check, and test
execution_preference.rationale: Small team; lightweight CI sufficient for local-only tool

project.goal: OEE analysis and cost-reduction opportunity reporting from SCADA exports
project.target_users: Manufacturing and maintenance engineers

backend.framework: Typer for CLI, DuckDB for analytics
backend.api: No HTTP API; file I/O only

security.auth: OS file permissions only

data.cache: None
data.formats: CSV input; CSV and PDF output

reasoner_reviews.product: The tool solves a real pain point: engineers spend hours manually aggregating SCADA CSVs in Excel to find top downtime contributors. The configurable hourly-rate YAML is essential because machine cost rates differ significantly across lines. PDF output should be deferred to v1.1 to keep initial scope tight.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: DuckDB is the right engine here: it reads CSV via read_csv_auto(), executes windowed aggregations on 500K events in under 5 seconds, and requires no server process. The main complexity is SCADA format variability across vendors; a parser plugin pattern with per-vendor schema mapping YAML will isolate this cleanly.
reasoner_reviews.security: Minimal attack surface for a local read-only tool. Key controls: pathlib.resolve() path traversal check on all input paths, 500MB file size guard to prevent OOM, no network calls verified by unit test. Reports should carry an INTERNAL CONFIDENTIAL header per data classification policy.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Windows 10/11 compatibility requires using pathlib throughout and avoiding Unix-specific path separators. DuckDB 1.0 is available as a Windows wheel. The 60-second SLA is easily met for typical SCADA export sizes; the integration test should assert analysis completes within 30 seconds on a 200K-row dataset.

backend.service_design: Single Python package oee_analyzer. Entry point: oee-analyze command. Subcommands: analyze (main pipeline), validate (schema pre-flight), config (view/update YAML defaults). Shared DuckDB context factory instantiated per analyze run.
backend.api_patterns: No HTTP API. CLI pattern: oee-analyze analyze --input ./exports/ --from 2025-01-01 --to 2025-12-31 --hourly-rate-config rates.yaml --classification-rules rules.yaml --output ./reports/ --format csv --top-n 10 --verbose.
backend.background_jobs: Single-process sequential execution: glob input files, parse to canonical schema, load into DuckDB in-memory, run SQL classification+aggregation pipeline, write output. No threads or async.
backend.failure_handling: Input validation errors reported with file path, line number, and column name. DuckDB errors re-raised as user-friendly AnalysisError. Unmatched fault codes counted and reported in stdout summary; --strict flag aborts if any unmatched.

security.auth_design: No authentication. OS file permissions govern input access.
security.abuse_prevention: Path traversal: all paths resolved via pathlib.Path.resolve() and asserted to be within --input directory. File size guard: reject files >500MB. No subprocess calls to external programs.
security.security_risks: Low risk. Tool is read-only and local-only. Main risk is reading wrong directory; mitigated by explicit --input path argument and validate subcommand pre-flight.

data.entities: equipment_assets (machine_id, machine_name, line_id, hourly_rate_usd loaded from YAML). downtime_events (event_id, machine_id, start_ts, end_ts, duration_min, fault_code, loss_category after classification, source_file). micro_stops (event_id, machine_id, occurred_at, duration_sec, fault_code). oee_snapshots (machine_id, shift_date, availability_pct, performance_pct, quality_pct, oee_pct). cost_categories (machine_id, loss_category, total_duration_min, annualized_cost_usd, event_count). opportunity_items (rank, machine_id, loss_category, annualized_cost_usd, event_count, recommended_tpm_action).
data.storage_design: DuckDB in-memory database created fresh per analysis run. Input CSVs loaded via read_csv_auto(). Optional --db-file flag writes DuckDB file to disk for post-run debugging. No persistent database by default.
data.migration_notes: No schema migrations. DuckDB schema created from input files each run.

devops.deployment_topology: Python wheel on internal PyPI mirror. pip install oee-analyzer on engineer workstations. PyInstaller single-binary planned for v1.1.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions matrix (ubuntu-latest, windows-latest): ruff check, mypy --strict, pytest. Integration test: run full analyze on 2000-row synthetic SCADA CSV, assert top opportunity rank matches expected machine and cost range. Tag-triggered release uploads wheel to internal PyPI via twine.
devops.cost_controls: Zero cloud infrastructure cost. CI on GitHub-hosted runners.
devops.devops_risks: Windows path handling bugs. Mitigated by CI matrix including Windows runner for integration tests.

target_output.thinking_summary: OEE CLI analysis tool: DuckDB as in-process engine reading SCADA CSV exports with configurable fault-code classification rules. Key design decisions: parser plugin pattern for SCADA vendor format variability, YAML-driven classification rules stored as separate files not hardcoded logic, DuckDB SQL templates in .sql files for maintainability. No server, no auth, no network — purely local file analysis with Rich-formatted stdout output.
target_output.title: OEE Cost-Reduction Analysis CLI (oee-analyzer)
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 CLI tool that ingests SCADA OEE export files, classifies downtime and micro-stop events into availability/performance/quality loss buckets using a YAML-driven fault-code rule engine, computes annualized cost impact per machine at configurable hourly rates using DuckDB SQL aggregations, and outputs a ranked top-10 opportunity report identifying priority TPM improvement targets. Runs fully offline on Windows workstations; 12-month analysis completes in under 60 seconds via DuckDB in-process query execution.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-process Python package structured as three layers: (1) Parser Layer with a BaseParser ABC and pluggable format readers (CsvScadaParser, ExcelScadaParser v1.1) that normalize vendor-specific SCADA exports into a canonical event schema; (2) Analysis Engine that loads normalized events into a DuckDB in-memory database and executes SQL templates for fault-code classification join, cost aggregation by machine and loss category, and opportunity ranking by annualized cost impact; (3) Output Layer writing CSV via csv.DictWriter and PDF via WeasyPrint Jinja2 template. Typer CLI provides three subcommands: analyze, validate, config. No network I/O, no subprocess calls, no server process.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Python 3.12. CLI: Typer 0.12 with Rich for progress bars and colored error output. Query engine: DuckDB 1.0 (in-process, reads CSV via read_csv_auto()). Config: PyYAML for hourly-rate and fault-code classification YAML files. SQL templates: loaded from package data via importlib.resources. PDF (v1.1): WeasyPrint with Jinja2 HTML template. Type checking: mypy strict. Linting: ruff. Packaging: pyproject.toml with hatchling; distributed via internal PyPI.
target_output.functional_feature_map: analyze subcommand: --input DIR, --from DATE, --to DATE, --machines COMMA_LIST, --hourly-rate-config YAML, --classification-rules YAML, --output DIR, --format csv|pdf|both, --top-n INT (default 10), --verbose, --log-file, --strict. validate subcommand: checks column names and types in input files against expected schema, reports mismatches per file with suggested corrections. config subcommand: shows or edits default YAML config files stored in ~/.oee-analyzer/.
target_output.system_components: CLI Entry Point (oee_analyzer.cli): Typer app with analyze, validate, config subcommands; argument validation and logging setup. Parser Registry (oee_analyzer.parsers): BaseParser ABC, CsvScadaParser with per-vendor schema mapping, ExcelScadaParser (v1.1). Analysis Engine (oee_analyzer.engine): DuckDB context factory, SQL templates in oee_analyzer/sql/ (classify_events.sql, aggregate_costs.sql, rank_opportunities.sql). Rules Engine (oee_analyzer.rules): YAML-driven fault_code to loss_category mapping with wildcard and prefix support. Report Writers (oee_analyzer.writers): CsvReportWriter, PdfReportWriter (v1.1). Config Manager (oee_analyzer.config): XDG-compliant config directory with package-data defaults.
target_output.workflows: Full Analysis Run: engineer runs 'oee-analyze analyze --input ./scada_exports/ --hourly-rate-config rates.yaml'. CLI globs input directory by extension, instantiates parser per file, normalizes rows to canonical schema (machine_id, event_ts, duration_min, fault_code, source_file). DuckDB loads all normalized events. SQL pipeline executes: (1) classify_events.sql LEFT JOINs fault_code against classification rules YAML table to assign loss_category; (2) aggregate_costs.sql GROUP BY machine_id + loss_category, sums duration_min, multiplies by hourly_rate/60 scaled to 365 days for annualized_cost_usd; (3) rank_opportunities.sql ORDER BY annualized_cost_usd DESC LIMIT top_n. Rich progress bar updates per step. CSV written to output directory. Validation Pre-Flight: engineer runs 'oee-analyze validate --input ./exports/' before analysis; tool checks each file for required columns (machine_id, start_ts, duration_min, fault_code), data type conformance, and timestamp parse success; reports errors per file with line numbers.
target_output.data_model: equipment_assets (machine_id VARCHAR PK, machine_name, line_id, hourly_rate_usd DECIMAL; loaded from rates YAML, not persisted). downtime_events (event_id VARCHAR, machine_id, start_ts TIMESTAMP, end_ts TIMESTAMP, duration_min DECIMAL, fault_code VARCHAR, loss_category VARCHAR assigned by rules join, source_file VARCHAR). micro_stops (event_id, machine_id, occurred_at TIMESTAMP, duration_sec SMALLINT, fault_code). oee_snapshots (machine_id, shift_date DATE, availability_pct DECIMAL, performance_pct DECIMAL, quality_pct DECIMAL, oee_pct DECIMAL). cost_categories (machine_id, loss_category ENUM planned_downtime/unplanned_breakdown/changeover/minor_stoppage/speed_loss/quality_loss, total_duration_min, annualized_cost_usd, event_count). opportunity_items (rank SMALLINT, machine_id, loss_category, annualized_cost_usd, event_count, avg_duration_min, recommended_tpm_action from rule YAML). All tables exist as DuckDB in-memory relations per run.
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API. CLI interface: 'oee-analyze analyze [OPTIONS]' key flags: --input PATH (required), --from TEXT YYYY-MM-DD (default 12 months ago), --to TEXT (default today), --machines TEXT comma-separated filter, --hourly-rate-config PATH, --classification-rules PATH, --output PATH (default ./oee_reports/), --format [csv|pdf|both] (default csv), --top-n INT (default 10), --verbose BOOL, --log-file PATH, --strict BOOL (abort on unmatched fault codes), --db-file PATH (optional DuckDB file for debug). 'oee-analyze validate --input PATH [--strict]'. 'oee-analyze config [show|edit] [--key KEY --value VALUE]'. Exit codes: 0 success, 1 input validation error, 2 analysis error, 3 output write error.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Path traversal mitigation: all input paths resolved via pathlib.Path.resolve() and asserted to be within the specified --input directory before open(). File size guard: input files >500MB rejected with actionable error. No credentials stored or transmitted. Zero network calls: unit test mocks socket.create_connection and asserts never called. Generated CSV reports include header row 'INTERNAL CONFIDENTIAL - OEE Analysis Report - generated YYYY-MM-DD' per internal data classification policy. Generated PDF (v1.1) includes same header in page footer.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Package installed via 'pip install oee-analyzer --index-url https://internal-pypi.plant.local/simple'. Requires Python 3.12 on Windows 10/11 or Linux. pyproject.toml entry_points registers 'oee-analyze' console script. GitHub Actions CI matrix (ubuntu-latest, windows-latest): ruff check, mypy --strict, pytest with coverage. Integration test generates 2000-row synthetic SCADA CSV with known downtime distribution (machine_c has highest changeover cost), runs 'oee-analyze analyze', asserts rank-1 is machine_c changeover within expected cost range. Release: tag vX.Y.Z triggers workflow that builds wheel and uploads to internal PyPI via twine with API token from GitHub secret. Windows-specific integration test validates pathlib.Path behavior and DuckDB wheel compatibility.
target_output.observability: --verbose emits per-step Rich progress: 'Parsing file 3/7 (machine_b_2025.csv) [14,823 events]', 'Classifying loss categories [2,341 matched, 47 unmatched fault codes]', 'Computing annualized costs', 'Writing CSV to ./reports/oee_20260324.csv'. --log-file writes JSON-structured entries per step with timestamps, file paths, row counts, and parse warnings. Final stdout summary table via Rich: top-5 opportunities with machine, loss category, and annualized cost. --strict mode prints unmatched fault code list and exits code 1 before writing output.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost. Runs on existing engineer workstations. DuckDB processes 500K-row SCADA dataset in 3-8 seconds on a standard i7-class workstation, well within the 60-second SLA. Package size target under 20MB including WeasyPrint (v1.1). No cloud services, no licenses, no subscriptions beyond internal PyPI server already in operation.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 v1.0 (6 weeks): CsvScadaParser, DuckDB analysis pipeline with SQL template files, YAML fault-code classification rules, cost calculation, CSV report output, validate and config subcommands, GitHub Actions CI with Windows runner, internal PyPI distribution. Phase 2 v1.1 (4 weeks): ExcelScadaParser using openpyxl, WeasyPrint PDF report with Jinja2 template, PyInstaller single-binary build, extended fault-code rule library covering 5 major SCADA vendor formats. Phase 3 v2.0 (roadmap): TUI mode with interactive threshold tuning via Textual, SAP PM integration for automated work order suggestion export, multi-site aggregation mode across shared network drives.
target_output.development_guidelines: mypy --strict throughout; no 'type: ignore' without inline comment explaining why. All parser functions have unit tests with fixture CSV files covering well-formed input, missing columns, malformed timestamps, and empty files. DuckDB SQL stored in oee_analyzer/sql/ as .sql files loaded via importlib.resources.read_text() — never inline f-string SQL. YAML rule files and rate configs are the only source of business logic for classification; no fault codes hardcoded in Python. Use pathlib.Path throughout; no os.path.join with string separators. Windows path compatibility verified by CI Windows runner on every PR.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: SCADA export format variability across vendors produces column name mismatches that silently drop events. Mitigation: parser plugin pattern with per-vendor schema mapping YAML; validate subcommand as mandatory pre-flight; --strict flag for zero-tolerance runs. Risk: YAML classification rules file missing fault codes present in new SCADA export batches. Mitigation: unmatched fault code count in every run summary; --strict abort mode. Tradeoff: DuckDB in-memory vs file-backed — in-memory chosen for simplicity and speed with --db-file escape hatch for debugging large datasets. Tradeoff: WeasyPrint PDF dependency adds ~15MB to package; deferred to v1.1 to keep initial install weight minimal.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected DuckDB over pandas: DuckDB SQL is more readable for multi-table aggregation joins at this scale and avoids DataFrame memory overhead. Chose Typer over argparse: automatic --help generation and type-validated arguments reduce boilerplate. Hourly rates in YAML config file rather than per-run CLI flags to avoid re-entry burden across repeated analysis runs. CSV prioritized over PDF for v1.0 to avoid WeasyPrint Windows compatibility issues during initial rollout.

metadata.notes: OEE CLI analysis tool: DuckDB-powered SCADA export analysis with Typer subcommands and YAML fault-code classification rules.

project.goal: Standardized PHM Python library for industrial time-series predictive maintenance
project.target_users: Data scientists and ML engineers

backend.framework: Pure Python with numpy/scipy

security.auth: None (public package)
security.compliance: pip-audit dependency scanning, Trusted Publisher PyPI releases

data.cache: None

constraints.dependencies: numpy, scipy mandatory; PyTorch optional extra

reasoner_reviews.product: The library fills a real gap: every PHM project reimplements RMS, kurtosis, and sliding-window builders from scratch. The fluent pipeline API design is the key user-experience differentiator. PHM benchmark report output will drive adoption among research teams comparing algorithms.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Pure Python with numpy/scipy is the correct foundation for broad compatibility. The model wrapper abstraction (unified predict_rul() interface over scikit-learn and PyTorch) is the most complex design surface and needs a well-specified Protocol class. Round 2 identified that the WindowDataset builder interface was not sufficiently flexible for variable-stride multivariate signals; this must be resolved in round 3.
reasoner_reviews.security: Open-source library security: pip-audit in CI catches transitive dependency vulnerabilities. Signed PyPI releases via Trusted Publishers prevent supply chain tampering. No credentials, no network calls, no data persistence — minimal attack surface.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: numpy<2.0 compatibility is a real constraint: some scipy FFT APIs changed in newer releases. The CI test matrix across Python 3.10/3.11/3.12 with pinned and unpinned numpy/scipy versions will catch regressions. Optional PyTorch extra correctly avoids forcing GPU dependency on all users.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Previous rounds did not adequately specify the public API contract for WindowDataset — specifically whether window boundaries are inclusive/exclusive, how NaN values in input signals are handled, and whether multivariate signals are supported in v0.2. These gaps will cause breaking API changes if not locked down now.

backend.service_design: Package structure: phm_toolkit/ (top-level). Subpackages: features/ (extractors), datasets/ (WindowDataset builder), models/ (wrappers), evaluation/ (metrics, report). Public API surface exported from phm_toolkit/__init__.py. All public classes and functions have type annotations and docstrings with numpy docstring format.
backend.api_patterns: Fluent pipeline: pipe = Pipeline([RmsExtractor(), KurtosisExtractor(), SpectralEntropyExtractor()]); features = pipe.transform(signal_array). WindowDataset(signal, labels, window_size=256, stride=32, label_horizon=10). ScikitWrapper(model).fit(dataset).predict_rul(signal). EvaluationReport(y_true, y_pred).summary().
backend.failure_handling: Input validation on all public API entry points: assert signal.ndim == 2 for multivariate input; raise ValueError with descriptive message for invalid window_size or stride combinations. NaN handling: default policy is raise on NaN in input signal; nan_policy='propagate' or 'omit' optional parameter added in v0.3.

security.auth_design: No authentication. Public open-source package.
security.secrets_management: No secrets. PyPI API token stored in GitHub Actions secret PYPI_TOKEN for Trusted Publisher flow.
security.security_risks: Supply chain: transitive numpy/scipy vulnerability. Mitigated by pip-audit job in CI on every PR and weekly scheduled scan. Typosquatting on PyPI name mitigated by publishing under established organization account.

data.entities: sensor_channels (represented as numpy ndarray columns in multivariate signal array). feature_extractors (stateless callables: RmsExtractor, KurtosisExtractor, SpectralEntropyExtractor, CrestFactorExtractor, BandPowerExtractor). window_datasets (WindowDataset class wrapping ndarray with windows, labels, and metadata dict). model_wrappers (ScikitWrapper, TorchWrapper Protocol adapters). evaluation_metrics (rul_mae, rul_rmse, fpr_at_threshold, fnr_at_threshold functions). rul_estimators (RULPredictor protocol with fit/predict_rul/evaluate interface).
data.schema_notes: All feature extractors implement FeatureExtractor Protocol: transform(signal: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray. WindowDataset implements __len__, __getitem__ for PyTorch DataLoader compatibility and sklearn-compatible numpy array export via to_numpy(). Public API locked to numpy ndarray; no pandas dependency in core.
data.retention_and_deletion: Library stores no data. All computation is stateless and in-memory.

devops.deployment_topology: PyPI package distribution via GitHub Actions Trusted Publisher (no stored API token). Read the Docs for Sphinx-generated API docs auto-built on tag push. GitHub Releases for changelog.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: (1) test matrix: Python 3.10/3.11/3.12 x numpy latest/pinned, runs pytest with coverage (90% gate), mypy --strict, ruff; (2) pip-audit for dependency vulnerability scan; (3) on semver tag: build wheel, upload to TestPyPI, smoke test install, upload to PyPI via Trusted Publisher, trigger Read the Docs build.
devops.cost_controls: Zero cost: PyPI free tier, GitHub Actions free tier, Read the Docs free tier for open-source.
devops.devops_risks: numpy/scipy API breaking changes across major versions. Mitigated by CI matrix with pinned and latest versions.

ISS-001.title: WindowDataset API contract underspecified for multivariate signals and NaN handling
ISS-001.detail: Public API does not define whether window_size is in samples or seconds, whether multivariate input is supported in v0.2, or how NaN values in input signal are handled. Leaving this undefined will force breaking API changes after initial release.

Addressed in prose but not in formal interface specification

ISS-002.title: predict_rul() Protocol definition missing for PyTorch wrapper compatibility
ISS-002.detail: The TorchWrapper model wrapper class does not define a formal typing.Protocol for predict_rul(), making it unclear what PyTorch nn.Module subclasses must implement to be compatible with the evaluation module.

history.note: Identified by architect_reasoner in round 2

ISS-003.detail: RMS and kurtosis extractors have only example-based tests; property-based tests with Hypothesis would catch edge cases such as zero-variance signals, single-sample windows, and all-NaN windows.

history.note: Flagged in critic review

For ISS-001, I will formally specify WindowDataset with typed __init__(signal: ndarray, labels: ndarray, window_size: int, stride: int, label_horizon: int, nan_policy: Literal['raise','propagate','omit']='raise') and document multivariate support (signal shape [n_samples, n_channels]). fix_report.action_taken: Formally specified WindowDataset typed interface: __init__(signal: np.ndarray, labels: np.ndarray, window_size: int, stride: int, label_horizon: int, nan_policy: Literal['raise','propagate','omit']='raise'). window_size is always in samples (integer). Multivariate input supported when signal.ndim==2 with shape [n_samples, n_channels]. NaN policy documented and enforced at construction time. API contract published in datasets module docstring and ADR-001 in docs/decisions/.
fix_report.expected_outcome: WindowDataset API is stable for v0.1 release; multivariate support and NaN handling behavior are unambiguous; no breaking changes required after release

fix_report.action_taken: Defined RULPredictor typing.Protocol in phm_toolkit/models/__init__.py with signatures: fit(dataset: WindowDataset) -> None, predict_rul(signal: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray, evaluate(y_true: np.ndarray, y_pred: np.ndarray) -> dict[str, float]. ScikitWrapper and TorchWrapper both implement this Protocol. Protocol class exported from top-level phm_toolkit namespace. runtime_checkable decorator applied for isinstance checks.
fix_report.expected_outcome: PyTorch model wrapper compatibility requirements are unambiguous; users can implement custom wrappers against the Protocol; mypy structural subtyping validates compliance

target_output.title: phm-toolkit: Industrial PHM Python Library v0.2
target_output.executive_summary: An open-source Python library (phm-toolkit) providing a standardized pipeline interface for industrial predictive maintenance models, covering signal feature extraction (RMS, kurtosis, spectral entropy, crest factor, band-power ratios), sliding-window dataset construction, unified scikit-learn and PyTorch model wrappers via a formal RULPredictor Protocol, and PHM-standard evaluation metrics (RUL MAE, RMSE, FPR/FNR at alerting threshold). Published on PyPI with numpy and scipy as the only mandatory dependencies. Round 3 resolves the WindowDataset API contract gap (multivariate support, NaN policy) and formalizes the RULPredictor Protocol.
target_output.architecture_overview: Stateless Python package with four subpackages: phm_toolkit.features (signal feature extractors implementing FeatureExtractor Protocol), phm_toolkit.datasets (WindowDataset class and Pipeline builder), phm_toolkit.models (RULPredictor Protocol, ScikitWrapper, TorchWrapper), phm_toolkit.evaluation (rul_mae, rul_rmse, fpr_at_threshold, fnr_at_threshold, EvaluationReport). All public classes and functions exported from phm_toolkit/__init__.py for flat import surface. No I/O, no network, no database. Operates entirely on caller-provided numpy ndarrays. Optional PyTorch dependency declared as extras_require['torch'] to avoid mandatory GPU dependency for scikit-learn users.
target_output.technology_stack: Core: Python 3.10-3.12, numpy>=1.24,<2.0, scipy>=1.11. Optional extras: phm-toolkit[torch] pulls PyTorch>=2.0. Dev: pytest>=8.0, hypothesis>=6.100 for property-based tests, pytest-benchmark for performance regression. Type checking: mypy>=1.9 --strict. Linting: ruff. Build: pyproject.toml with hatchling. Docs: Sphinx with autodoc and numpy docstring extension on Read the Docs. Release: GitHub Actions Trusted Publisher to PyPI. Dependency scanning: pip-audit on PR and weekly cron.
target_output.functional_feature_map: FeatureExtractor Protocol: transform(signal: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray; implemented by RmsExtractor, KurtosisExtractor, SpectralEntropyExtractor, CrestFactorExtractor, BandPowerExtractor(bands: list[tuple[float,float]], sample_rate: float). Pipeline: __init__(steps: list[FeatureExtractor]); transform(signal) chains extractors and returns feature matrix. WindowDataset: __init__(signal: np.ndarray [n_samples, n_channels], labels: np.ndarray, window_size: int, stride: int, label_horizon: int, nan_policy: Literal['raise','propagate','omit']='raise'); __len__(); __getitem__(idx) -> tuple[np.ndarray, float]; to_numpy() -> tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray] for sklearn. RULPredictor Protocol (runtime_checkable): fit(dataset: WindowDataset) -> None; predict_rul(signal: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray; evaluate(y_true, y_pred) -> dict[str,float]. ScikitWrapper(estimator: BaseEstimator) and TorchWrapper(module: nn.Module, device: str='cpu') both implement RULPredictor. Evaluation: rul_mae, rul_rmse, fpr_at_threshold(y_true, scores, threshold), fnr_at_threshold; EvaluationReport.summary() returns dict and .to_markdown() for README integration.
target_output.system_components: phm_toolkit/features/__init__.py: FeatureExtractor Protocol, Pipeline class. phm_toolkit/features/time_domain.py: RmsExtractor, KurtosisExtractor, CrestFactorExtractor. phm_toolkit/features/frequency_domain.py: SpectralEntropyExtractor (scipy.fft), BandPowerExtractor (scipy.signal.welch). phm_toolkit/datasets/__init__.py: WindowDataset class, nan_policy validation logic. phm_toolkit/models/__init__.py: RULPredictor Protocol (runtime_checkable), ScikitWrapper, TorchWrapper (guarded by TYPE_CHECKING for optional torch import). phm_toolkit/evaluation/__init__.py: metric functions, EvaluationReport. tests/: pytest unit tests and Hypothesis property-based tests per subpackage. docs/: Sphinx source, ADR-001 (WindowDataset API contract), ADR-002 (RULPredictor Protocol).
target_output.workflows: Data Scientist Feature Extraction Workflow: user imports Pipeline, RmsExtractor, KurtosisExtractor, SpectralEntropyExtractor; constructs Pipeline([RmsExtractor(), KurtosisExtractor(), SpectralEntropyExtractor()]); calls pipe.transform(vibration_array) where vibration_array has shape [n_samples, n_channels]; receives feature matrix [n_windows, n_features]. WindowDataset Construction and Model Training: user creates WindowDataset(signal=vibration_array, labels=rul_array, window_size=256, stride=32, label_horizon=10, nan_policy='raise'); passes dataset to ScikitWrapper(RandomForestRegressor()).fit(dataset); calls wrapper.predict_rul(new_signal) to get RUL predictions; passes y_true and y_pred to EvaluationReport and calls .summary() to get MAE, RMSE, FPR, FNR dict for benchmark comparison.
target_output.data_model: sensor_channels: represented as columns of numpy ndarray with shape [n_samples, n_channels]; column ordering is caller-defined and documented in user guide. feature_extractors: stateless Protocol callables; RmsExtractor computes sqrt(mean(x**2)) per window per channel; KurtosisExtractor computes scipy.stats.kurtosis per window; SpectralEntropyExtractor computes normalized spectral entropy via scipy.fft; BandPowerExtractor integrates Welch PSD over user-specified frequency bands. window_datasets: WindowDataset stores reference to original signal ndarray and computes windows lazily in __getitem__ to minimize memory footprint; supports multivariate signal [n_samples, n_channels] from round 3 onwards. model_wrappers: ScikitWrapper stores fitted BaseEstimator; predict_rul() calls estimator.predict() on windowed feature matrix; TorchWrapper stores nn.Module and device; predict_rul() calls module.forward() with torch.no_grad(). evaluation_metrics: rul_mae and rul_rmse operate on aligned y_true/y_pred ndarrays; fpr_at_threshold and fnr_at_threshold accept anomaly score array and scalar threshold.
target_output.api_design: Public Python API (no HTTP). Top-level exports from phm_toolkit/__init__.py: Pipeline, RmsExtractor, KurtosisExtractor, SpectralEntropyExtractor, CrestFactorExtractor, BandPowerExtractor, WindowDataset, RULPredictor, ScikitWrapper, TorchWrapper, rul_mae, rul_rmse, fpr_at_threshold, fnr_at_threshold, EvaluationReport. Versioned via semver in pyproject.toml; breaking changes only on major version bump. ADR-001 documents WindowDataset API stability guarantee: window_size is samples (integer), stride is samples (integer), multivariate signal [n_samples, n_channels] supported from v0.2, nan_policy added in v0.3. ADR-002 documents RULPredictor Protocol stability guarantee: fit/predict_rul/evaluate signatures frozen for v1.0. Changelog in CHANGELOG.md updated on every release.
target_output.security_and_compliance: No network calls: verified by unit test that patches socket and asserts it is never invoked. No credential storage. Signed PyPI releases via GitHub Actions Trusted Publishers (OIDC-based, no stored API tokens). pip-audit job runs on every PR and weekly cron scanning numpy, scipy, and all dev dependencies for CVEs. Package published under phm-toolkit organization account on PyPI to prevent name squatting. Source code on public GitHub; MIT license. No telemetry or usage tracking of any kind in library code.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI workflows: (1) test.yml: triggered on PR and push to main; matrix: Python 3.10 x numpy-pinned, Python 3.11 x numpy-latest, Python 3.12 x numpy-latest; runs ruff check, mypy --strict, pytest --cov --cov-fail-under=90; (2) audit.yml: weekly cron pip-audit scan; (3) release.yml: triggered on semver tag vX.Y.Z; builds wheel via python -m build, uploads to TestPyPI, runs install smoke test 'python -c "from phm_toolkit import Pipeline, RmsExtractor; print(Pipeline([RmsExtractor()]).transform.__doc__)"', uploads to PyPI via Trusted Publisher, triggers Read the Docs build via webhook. Docs: Sphinx autodoc pulls from numpy-style docstrings; ADR documents in docs/decisions/; hosted free on Read the Docs.
target_output.observability: Library emits Python logging at DEBUG level via logging.getLogger('phm_toolkit'). No metrics, no telemetry, no external calls. pytest-benchmark suite in tests/benchmarks/ measures transform throughput for RmsExtractor and SpectralEntropyExtractor on 10K-sample signals; benchmark results committed to benchmarks/results/ and compared in CI via pytest-benchmark --compare to detect performance regressions >10%. CI coverage report published to Codecov on every PR.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost. PyPI free tier, GitHub Actions free tier (2000 min/month for public repo), Read the Docs free tier for open-source. Wheel build takes under 60 seconds. Full CI matrix (3 Python versions) completes in under 5 minutes. No cloud compute required; all CI runs on GitHub-hosted runners.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase v0.1 (4 weeks, complete): RmsExtractor, KurtosisExtractor, SpectralEntropyExtractor, Pipeline, WindowDataset (univariate), ScikitWrapper, rul_mae/rmse, PyPI publication, CI matrix. Phase v0.2 (current, 4 weeks): Resolve ISS-001 (WindowDataset multivariate + NaN policy) and ISS-002 (RULPredictor Protocol), add Hypothesis property-based tests for all feature extractors (ISS-003), CrestFactorExtractor and BandPowerExtractor, EvaluationReport with to_markdown(). Phase v0.3 (roadmap): TorchWrapper with PyTorch optional extra, LSTM/TCN model wrapper examples, CMAPSS benchmark dataset loader, fpr_at_threshold and fnr_at_threshold anomaly metrics, Sphinx documentation site.
target_output.development_guidelines: mypy --strict with no 'type: ignore' without explanation comment. All public API functions and classes have numpy-style docstrings with Parameters, Returns, Raises, and Examples sections. Feature extractor unit tests use golden output fixtures validated against hand-computed values (e.g., RMS of [1, 2, 3] = sqrt(14/3)). Hypothesis property-based tests for WindowDataset: assert len(dataset) == floor((n_samples - window_size) / stride) + 1 for any valid (n_samples, window_size, stride) combination. TorchWrapper import guarded with 'if TYPE_CHECKING' to keep torch import optional at runtime. SQL templates — not applicable; no SQL in library. ADR documents for WindowDataset and RULPredictor Protocol committed to docs/decisions/ before v0.2 tag.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: numpy 2.0 API changes break FFT and signal processing calls in SpectralEntropyExtractor and BandPowerExtractor. Mitigation: numpy<2.0 pin in requires-python metadata; CI matrix tests against numpy 1.24 and 1.26 pinned. Risk: WindowDataset lazy window computation in __getitem__ is slower for PyTorch DataLoader multi-worker mode due to repeated slicing. Mitigation: to_numpy() eager export for batch use cases; lazy mode optimal for single-threaded sklearn. Tradeoff: scipy-only vs custom FFT implementation in SpectralEntropyExtractor — scipy chosen for correctness and maintenance over performance; acceptable for library use case. Tradeoff: RULPredictor Protocol (structural subtyping) vs abstract base class (nominal) — Protocol chosen for flexibility; users can make any class compatible without inheriting from library base class.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-001 resolved: WindowDataset API contract locked in ADR-001 with window_size in samples, multivariate [n_samples, n_channels] supported from v0.2, nan_policy parameter with raise/propagate/omit options. ISS-002 resolved: RULPredictor typing.Protocol with runtime_checkable decorator published in phm_toolkit/models/__init__.py; both ScikitWrapper and TorchWrapper verified via mypy structural check. ISS-003 resolved: Hypothesis property-based tests added for RmsExtractor (zero-variance, single-sample, constant signal), KurtosisExtractor (Gaussian distribution kurtosis asymptote), and WindowDataset length invariant.

metadata.notes: Round 3 PHM library SDK: resolves WindowDataset API contract and RULPredictor Protocol; Hypothesis property-based tests added.

project_goal.value: Deploy a high-throughput time-series ingestion and query infrastructure for a food and beverage plant historian, collecting process variable data from 8,000 OPC-UA tags at one-second resolution from PLCs and storing it in InfluxDB for querying by process engineers and quality analysts
project_goal.rationale: Replaces aging OSIsoft PI historian approaching end-of-life with an open-source stack owned by the plant

target_users.value: Process engineers querying historical trends and alarm setpoints; quality analysts reviewing product batch conformance; instrument maintenance team reviewing data quality events
target_users.rationale: Three distinct query personas with different access patterns identified in workshops

project_class.rationale: Platform and environment architecture: Kubernetes, Kafka, InfluxDB, OPC-UA connectors; no application-layer UI delivered by this project

capabilities.value: backend, data, realtime, devops, integrations, analytics
capabilities.rationale: OPC-UA integration, Kafka streaming, InfluxDB time-series, Grafana analytics, GitOps devops

complexity_level.rationale: 8,000 OPC-UA tags at 1-second resolution = 8,000 writes/second sustained; multi-tier retention; data quality validation pipeline

risk_level.rationale: Internal plant data; no external exposure; but OPC-UA interface with production PLCs requires careful network segmentation

data_sensitivity.rationale: Process variable data is internal operational data; not personal, financial, or health data

external_exposure.rationale: System runs on plant intranet; no internet-facing endpoints

access_model.value: Keycloak RBAC: process_engineer (read Grafana dashboards, REST API query), quality_analyst (read batch conformance views), instrument_tech (read data quality events), admin (Grafana admin, Keycloak admin), service_account (OPC-UA connector write-only to Kafka)
access_model.rationale: Least-privilege access for each persona; write access only for automated connectors

feature_scope.value: OPC-UA client connectors polling 8,000 tags at 1-second resolution, Kafka-based ingestion pipeline with data quality validation (range checks, stale value detection), InfluxDB time-series storage with hot/warm/cold retention tiers, Grafana dashboards for trends and alarm setpoints, REST query API for batch conformance integration, data quality event store for instrument maintenance
feature_scope.rationale: Full scope defined in migration requirements workshop

mvp_scope.value: OPC-UA connectors for 2,000 priority tags, Kafka ingestion, InfluxDB hot tier, Grafana trend dashboards for process engineers; remaining 6,000 tags and cold tier in phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: Pilot on high-value process lines first to validate accuracy against legacy PI historian

security_baseline.value: Keycloak authentication for all human access; OPC-UA connector service accounts with write-only Kafka ACLs; TLS on all inter-service communication; network policy restricting OPC-UA connector pods to PLC network CIDR only; audit log for all Keycloak auth events
security_baseline.rationale: OT/IT boundary security and internal access control requirements

frontend_stack.value: None (Grafana serves as the analytics frontend; no custom web app delivered by this infrastructure project)
frontend_stack.rationale: Grafana sufficient for process engineer and quality analyst dashboard needs

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with custom OPC-UA ingestion service; Kafka 3.6 (MSK-style 3-broker cluster on-premises); InfluxDB 2.7; REST query API in Go with Gin
backend_stack.rationale: Go for high-throughput low-latency ingestion; Kafka for buffering and replay; InfluxDB for native time-series compression

data_platform.value: InfluxDB 2.7 (hot: 30-day SSD retention, 8,000 writes/sec target); Kafka 3.6 (ingestion buffer, 7-day retention for replay); cold tier via Parquet export to on-premises S3-compatible object store (MinIO)
data_platform.rationale: InfluxDB native TSM engine handles 8,000 writes/sec on 3-node cluster; Kafka provides replay safety net

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal Kubernetes cluster on-premises (3 control plane nodes, 6 worker nodes) with dedicated SSD storage nodes for InfluxDB; no cloud dependency
hosting_target.rationale: Plant network air-gap policy; all infrastructure on-premises

privacy_retention_policy.value: Hot tier: 30 days in InfluxDB SSD; warm tier: 90 days in InfluxDB HDD via downsampling to 10-second resolution; cold tier: 5 years as Parquet on MinIO; data quality events: 2 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Internal data retention policy and potential regulatory audit requirements

future_scope.value: Alarm setpoint historian, batch record integration with MES, REST API expansion for third-party analytics tools, self-service Grafana dashboard builder for process engineers
future_scope.rationale: Phase 3 roadmap items post full-tag rollout

constraints.value: Bare-metal Kubernetes on-premises only; no cloud services; InfluxDB must sustain 8,000 writes/second on production hardware; OPC-UA connector must not add more than 500ms jitter to tag polling
constraints.rationale: Plant network isolation, performance SLA, and real-time polling constraints

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for Kafka broker health, InfluxDB write throughput, OPC-UA connector polling lag, data quality event rate; PagerDuty for Kafka lag >10,000 messages or InfluxDB write error rate >0.1%
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing plant monitoring stack; PagerDuty for on-call escalation

execution_preference.value: GitOps with Flux CD; all Kubernetes manifests in Git; infrastructure changes via PR with approval; zero manual kubectl applies in production
execution_preference.rationale: GitOps ensures audit trail and reproducibility for plant infrastructure changes

project.goal: High-throughput OPC-UA historian infrastructure: 8,000 tags at 1-second resolution into InfluxDB
project.target_users: Process engineers, quality analysts, instrument maintenance team
project.timeline: MVP (2,000 tags) in 12 weeks; full 8,000 tags in 20 weeks

backend.framework: Custom OPC-UA ingestion service + Gin REST API
backend.api: REST query API at /api/v1/timeseries; no HTTP for ingestion path

security.auth: Keycloak RBAC
security.encryption: TLS on all services; OPC-UA SecureChannel
security.compliance: Internal access control policy; network policies for OT/IT segmentation

data.cache: Redis 7 for query result caching

reasoner_reviews.product: The PI historian replacement is a high-stakes migration: process engineers depend on tag trend data for daily process monitoring. The MVP on 2,000 priority tags is the right approach to validate data accuracy against PI before decommissioning. The REST query API for batch conformance integration is a key differentiator over PI.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: InfluxDB 3-node cluster with dedicated SSD storage nodes is the right configuration for 8,000 writes/second sustained load. The OPC-UA connector design needs explicit specification of back-pressure handling when Kafka is unavailable. Previous rounds lacked detail on the data quality validation pipeline architecture — specifically the stream processor that runs range checks and stale value detection before writing to InfluxDB.
reasoner_reviews.security: OPC-UA connector pods must be restricted to PLC network CIDR via Kubernetes NetworkPolicy to prevent lateral movement if a connector pod is compromised. Keycloak service accounts for connector write-only Kafka ACLs prevent read access to historian data from the connector layer. TLS on Kafka inter-broker communication is mandatory given the shared network.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 8,000 writes/second InfluxDB target requires the TSM engine batch write path with line protocol batching at the connector layer — individual writes at 1Hz per tag will not reach this throughput. Connector must buffer and batch-write at 500ms intervals using InfluxDB Go client write API. OPC-UA SecureChannel overhead adds latency; connector must use SignAndEncrypt security mode with pre-provisioned certificates.

backend.service_design: Three Go services: OpcUaConnector (polls OPC-UA nodes, batches tag values, writes to Kafka); DataQualityProcessor (Kafka consumer, runs range checks and stale detection, writes conforming records to InfluxDB and anomalous records to data_quality_events topic); QueryAPI (Gin REST service wrapping InfluxDB Flux query API and Redis cache).
backend.api_patterns: QueryAPI REST at /api/v1/timeseries: GET /tags (list available OPC-UA tags with metadata), GET /tags/{tag_id}/values?from=&to=&resolution= (Flux query with downsampling), GET /batch/{batch_id}/conformance (batch conformance view joining process variables against spec limits).
backend.failure_handling: OpcUaConnector: if Kafka is unavailable, buffer tag values in memory ring buffer (max 60 seconds) and write to local SQLite WAL for replay on reconnection. DataQualityProcessor: dead-letter topic for records failing range check parsing. InfluxDB write failure: exponential backoff with 3 retries, then dead-letter to Kafka historian-dlq topic.

security.auth_design: Keycloak realm for historian with client credentials for service accounts (OpcUaConnector, DataQualityProcessor write-only Kafka ACL, QueryAPI read-only InfluxDB). Human users: process_engineer, quality_analyst, instrument_tech roles with Keycloak JWT validation at QueryAPI and Grafana.
security.secrets_management: OPC-UA server certificates and Keycloak client secrets stored in Kubernetes Secrets encrypted at rest via etcd encryption provider. Flux CD sealed-secrets for GitOps-safe secret management.
security.security_risks: OPC-UA connector compromise could allow PLC command injection if OT network access is not restricted. Mitigated by Kubernetes NetworkPolicy allowing connector pods egress only to PLC network CIDR and Kafka broker IP. OPC-UA SecureChannel certificate pinning prevents MITM.

data.entities: opc_ua_tags (tag_id, node_id, data_type, eu_range_low, eu_range_high, description, plc_source). process_variables (InfluxDB measurement: tag_id as field key, value FLOAT, quality_code INT, ingested_at TIMESTAMP, source_plc). data_quality_events (tag_id, event_type ENUM range_violation/stale_value/parse_error, observed_value, threshold, occurred_at). historian_records (InfluxDB downsampled warm tier: mean/min/max/stddev per 10-second window). retention_policies (hot: 30d SSD, warm: 90d HDD 10s resolution, cold: 5yr MinIO Parquet).
data.storage_design: InfluxDB 3-node cluster on dedicated NVMe SSD nodes. Bucket 'historian-hot' with 30d retention and ShardDuration 1d. Continuous query task downsamples to 'historian-warm' bucket at 10-second resolution with 90d retention. MinIO export: nightly Parquet export job writes daily Parquet partitioned by measurement date and tag_id prefix.
data.retention_and_deletion: Automatic via InfluxDB bucket retention policies. Cold tier MinIO: 5-year object lifecycle policy. Data quality events in separate InfluxDB bucket with 2-year retention.

devops.deployment_topology: Bare-metal Kubernetes 9-node production cluster: 3 control plane, 3 worker (general), 3 storage (NVMe SSD dedicated to InfluxDB StatefulSet). InfluxDB deployed as 3-replica StatefulSet with PVC on NVMe StorageClass. Kafka 3-broker StatefulSet with JBOD disks. Flux CD GitOps: all manifests in historian-infra Git repo; Flux watches main branch and reconciles.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: Go unit tests and integration tests (OpcUaConnector with mock OPC-UA server, DataQualityProcessor with embedded Kafka), Docker build and push to internal Harbor registry, Helm chart lint and dry-run. Merge to main triggers Flux reconciliation in staging. Production deploy via PR to production-values branch with security team approval.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping Kafka JMX exporter, InfluxDB /metrics endpoint, Go service custom metrics. Grafana dashboards: Kafka consumer lag per partition, InfluxDB write throughput vs 8K target, OpcUaConnector polling lag histogram, data quality event rate by tag and event type. PagerDuty: Kafka consumer lag >10K, InfluxDB write error rate >0.1%, OpcUaConnector heartbeat loss.
devops.rollback_strategy: Flux CD GitOps rollback: revert Git commit in production-values branch; Flux reconciles within 60 seconds. InfluxDB schema changes: Flux task definitions in Git; backward-compatible field additions only; no tag deletion without data migration runbook.

ISS-001.title: DataQualityProcessor stream architecture not specified
ISS-001.detail: Previous rounds described data quality validation in prose but did not specify whether it runs as a Kafka Streams job, a custom Go consumer, or a separate service. Architecture ambiguity blocked implementation planning.

Specified as custom Go Kafka consumer service in round 2 plan

ISS-002.title: OpcUaConnector back-pressure handling unspecified for Kafka unavailability
ISS-002.detail: No design exists for what the OpcUaConnector does when Kafka brokers are unreachable: whether it drops tag values, buffers in memory, or writes to a local WAL. Dropping values would create historian gaps unacceptable for process monitoring continuity.

history.note: Flagged in constraints review
Memory buffer mentioned but WAL design and replay mechanism not specified
