target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 must resolve ISS-002 (OpcUaConnector back-pressure) and ISS-003 (InfluxDB throughput validation). For ISS-002, I will specify a two-tier buffer: in-memory circular ring buffer (60-second at 8K/s = ~480K entries) spilling to SQLite WAL file with 10-minute retention and a replay job that drains the WAL on Kafka reconnection. For ISS-003, I will specify the InfluxDB line protocol batch write strategy (500ms batch windows, 4,000-point batch size via Go client WriteAPI) and add a load test acceptance criterion to the staging pipeline using influx write with synthetic tag streams.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified two-tier back-pressure design for OpcUaConnector: (1) in-memory circular ring buffer capped at 480,000 tag-value entries (approximately 60 seconds at 8,000 tags/sec); (2) when ring buffer is full or Kafka unavailable >5 seconds, spill to local SQLite WAL file at /var/historian/connector-wal.db with 10-minute maximum retention (older entries evicted); (3) WalReplayJob goroutine wakes every 10 seconds on Kafka reconnection, drains WAL in chronological order, resubmits to Kafka with original timestamps preserved. WAL file size limit 512MB; if exceeded, oldest entries dropped and data gap event written to Prometheus counter historian_connector_wal_drop_total.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Historian gaps limited to events older than 10 minutes plus WAL drain time; acceptable for food production process monitoring; Prometheus counter provides visibility into any drop events

fix_report.action_taken: Specified InfluxDB write strategy: OpcUaConnector uses InfluxDB Go client WriteAPI in non-blocking mode with 500ms flush interval and MaxBatchSize=4000 points; this produces 16 batch writes/second at 8,000 tags/sec sustained. Staging load test acceptance criterion added to GitLab CI: influx-write-test job generates 8,000 synthetic tag streams for 10 minutes on staging cluster and asserts p99 write latency <200ms and write error rate <0.01%. InfluxDB TSM engine WAL flush interval set to 10ms for NVMe write optimization.
fix_report.expected_outcome: InfluxDB write throughput target validated on staging hardware before production rollout; batch write strategy prevents TCP connection saturation from per-tag individual writes

target_output.title: Food & Beverage Plant Process Historian Infrastructure
target_output.executive_summary: An on-premises Kubernetes infrastructure project replacing an end-of-life OSIsoft PI historian with an open-source stack: OPC-UA connector services polling 8,000 process variable tags at one-second resolution, Kafka ingestion buffer with data quality validation, InfluxDB 2.7 three-node cluster on NVMe SSDs targeting 8,000 writes/second sustained, Grafana dashboards for process engineers and quality analysts, and a REST query API for batch conformance integration. Round 3 resolves OpcUaConnector back-pressure design (two-tier ring buffer and SQLite WAL replay) and InfluxDB write throughput validation (500ms batch writes and staging load test gate). GitOps via Flux CD with all infrastructure declared in Git.
target_output.architecture_overview: Five-layer architecture on bare-metal Kubernetes. OT Layer: OPC-UA SecureChannel connections from OpcUaConnector pods (restricted to PLC CIDR via NetworkPolicy) poll 8,000 tag nodes every second; connector batches tag values into 500ms windows of 4,000 points and publishes to Kafka 'historian-raw' topic using InfluxDB line protocol. Kafka Layer: 3-broker cluster with 32 partitions on historian-raw topic; DataQualityProcessor consumer group reads, validates range and staleness, writes conforming records to InfluxDB via WriteAPI and anomalous records to 'historian-quality-events' topic. InfluxDB Layer: 3-node StatefulSet on NVMe StorageClass; TSM engine with 10ms WAL flush; hot bucket 30d/1s resolution, warm bucket 90d/10s resolution via continuous query task, nightly Parquet export to MinIO cold. Query Layer: QueryAPI (Go Gin) serves Grafana data source and REST clients with Redis 7 result caching for range queries. Operations Layer: Flux CD GitOps, Prometheus/Grafana monitoring, PagerDuty alerting. OpcUaConnector back-pressure: two-tier buffer (in-memory 60s ring buffer then SQLite WAL 10-min retention) with WalReplayJob on Kafka reconnection.
target_output.technology_stack: Ingestion: Go 1.22, opcua Go library (gopcua/opcua), InfluxDB Go client v2 WriteAPI. Message broker: Kafka 3.6 with 3 brokers and SASL/TLS. Time-series store: InfluxDB 2.7 with Flux query language. Cache: Redis 7 for QueryAPI result caching. Cold storage: MinIO (S3-compatible) with Parquet via Apache Arrow Go library. Container platform: Kubernetes 1.29 bare-metal. GitOps: Flux CD v2 with sealed-secrets. Monitoring: Prometheus, Grafana 10, kafka-exporter, influxdb-exporter. Auth: Keycloak 24. CI/CD: GitLab CI with Harbor registry.
target_output.functional_feature_map: OpcUaConnector: OPC-UA SecureChannel polling with SignAndEncrypt security mode; 500ms batch window producing 4,000-point line protocol writes to Kafka; in-memory ring buffer (480K entries) and SQLite WAL for Kafka back-pressure; WalReplayJob goroutine for replay on reconnection. DataQualityProcessor: Kafka consumer group; per-tag range check against eu_range_low/eu_range_high from tag registry; stale value detection (value unchanged for >30s on analog tags); conforming records to InfluxDB WriteAPI; anomalous records to quality-events topic. QueryAPI: GET /api/v1/tags (tag registry), GET /api/v1/tags/{id}/values?from=&to=&resolution= (Flux query with optional downsampling), GET /api/v1/batches/{batch_id}/conformance (SPC-style conformance view). Grafana: process engineer trend dashboards with tag search, alarm setpoint overlay, shift comparison; quality analyst batch conformance heatmap. MinIO Exporter: nightly CronJob exports previous day InfluxDB bucket to Parquet partitioned by date and tag prefix.
target_output.system_components: OpcUaConnector (Go, 3 replicas on OT-network worker nodes): gopcua/opcua client, Kafka producer, in-memory ring buffer, SQLite WAL at /var/historian/connector-wal.db, WalReplayJob goroutine. DataQualityProcessor (Go, 3 replicas): Kafka consumer group historian-dqp, InfluxDB WriteAPI with 500ms flush and MaxBatchSize=4000, Kafka producer for quality-events. QueryAPI (Go Gin, 2 replicas): InfluxDB Flux query client, Redis 7 cache with 60s TTL for range queries, Keycloak JWT middleware. InfluxDB StatefulSet (3 nodes, NVMe PVC): buckets historian-hot, historian-warm, historian-quality; continuous query tasks for warm downsampling; TSM WAL flush 10ms. Kafka StatefulSet (3 brokers, JBOD HDD PVC): historian-raw (32 partitions, 7d retention), historian-quality-events, historian-dlq. MinIO (4-node erasure set on HDD PVC): historian-cold bucket with 5y lifecycle. Redis StatefulSet (1 replica with AOF persistence). Prometheus + Grafana (monitoring namespace). Keycloak (2-replica deployment with PostgreSQL backend).
target_output.workflows: Tag Ingestion Path: OpcUaConnector subscribes to 8,000 OPC-UA node IDs on PLC OPC-UA servers; MonitoredItem callbacks fire at 1-second intervals; values accumulated in 500ms batch window; when batch reaches 4,000 points or 500ms elapses, line protocol batch published to Kafka historian-raw with tag_id, value, quality_code, and timestamp fields. DataQualityProcessor reads historian-raw partitions; for each record looks up tag eu_range in in-memory tag registry (refreshed hourly from InfluxDB tag metadata bucket); writes conforming records to InfluxDB historian-hot bucket via WriteAPI; stale value events written to historian-quality-events Kafka topic. OpcUaConnector Kafka Back-Pressure: on Kafka produce error or broker unavailability, connector writes to in-memory ring buffer (cap 480K entries, 60s at 8K/s); if ring buffer >80% full, spills to SQLite WAL at /var/historian/connector-wal.db; WAL entries older than 10 minutes evicted with historian_connector_wal_drop_total Prometheus counter increment; WalReplayJob drains WAL in chronological order on successful Kafka reconnection within 10 seconds.
target_output.data_model: opc_ua_tags (tag_id VARCHAR PK, node_id VARCHAR, data_type ENUM float/integer/boolean, eu_range_low FLOAT, eu_range_high FLOAT, engineering_unit VARCHAR, description VARCHAR, plc_source VARCHAR, active BOOL) stored in InfluxDB metadata bucket and in-memory hash map in DataQualityProcessor. process_variables (InfluxDB measurement 'process_var': tag_id as tag key, value FLOAT as field, quality_code INT as field, source_plc as tag, _time TIMESTAMP) in historian-hot bucket with 30d retention. historian_records (InfluxDB measurement 'process_var_10s' in historian-warm: mean_value, min_value, max_value, stddev_value per 10-second window, generated by continuous query task). data_quality_events (InfluxDB measurement 'quality_event': tag_id, event_type ENUM range_violation/stale_value/parse_error, observed_value, threshold, occurred_at) in historian-quality bucket with 2y retention. cold_parquet (MinIO: historian-cold/YYYY-MM-DD/tag_prefix/data.parquet partitioned by date and tag_id prefix character, Apache Arrow schema matching process_variables fields).
target_output.api_design: QueryAPI REST (Go Gin, /api/v1, Keycloak JWT Bearer required): GET /tags — returns tag registry (tag_id, node_id, engineering_unit, description); paginated with ?limit=&offset=. GET /tags/{tag_id}/values?from=RFC3339&to=RFC3339&resolution=1s|10s|1m|1h — executes Flux range+filter+aggregateWindow query; cached in Redis with key hash(tag_id+from+to+resolution), TTL 60s for recent queries, 3600s for historical. GET /batches/{batch_id}/conformance — joins process variables against batch time window and spec limits from MES API, returns SPC summary (mean, sigma, Cpk). GET /quality-events?tag_id=&from=&to=&type= — queries historian-quality bucket. All responses JSON with pagination envelope. Error format: {error: string, code: int, tag_id: string}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Keycloak 24 realm 'historian': process_engineer role (QueryAPI read, Grafana viewer), quality_analyst (QueryAPI read, Grafana viewer), instrument_tech (quality-events read only), admin (Grafana admin, Keycloak admin). Service accounts: opcua-connector (Kafka write-only ACL on historian-raw), dqp-service (Kafka read ACL historian-raw, write ACL quality-events, InfluxDB write-only historian-hot and historian-quality). Kubernetes NetworkPolicy: OpcUaConnector pods allow egress only to PLC CIDR 10.10.5.0/24 and Kafka service CIDR; deny all other egress. OPC-UA SecureChannel: SignAndEncrypt security mode with server certificates pinned in connector ConfigMap. TLS: mutual TLS on Kafka inter-broker; TLS termination at Keycloak and QueryAPI. Sealed-secrets for all Kubernetes Secrets in Git repo.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cluster: 9-node bare-metal Kubernetes 1.29. Node pools: 3 control-plane (8 core, 32GB RAM), 3 general-worker (16 core, 64GB RAM, for OpcUaConnector, DataQualityProcessor, QueryAPI, Kafka), 3 storage-worker (32 core, 128GB RAM, 4x NVMe 2TB each for InfluxDB StatefulSet PVC). GitLab CI pipeline: (1) Go unit + integration tests with mock OPC-UA server (gopcua testserver) and embedded Kafka (test containers), (2) Docker build and push to internal Harbor, (3) Helm chart lint and kube-score manifest validation, (4) staging deploy via Flux imagepolicy update, (5) staging acceptance: influx-write-test job injecting 8,000 synthetic tag streams for 10 minutes asserting p99 write latency <200ms and error rate <0.01%, (6) production deploy via PR to production-values branch with operations team approval. Flux CD: kustomize overlays per environment; imagepolicy auto-update for patch releases; sealed-secrets for credential management.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapers: kafka-exporter (lag per consumer group and partition), influxdb-exporter (write throughput, series cardinality, TSM compaction), custom Go metrics from OpcUaConnector (historian_connector_poll_lag_ms histogram, historian_connector_wal_size_bytes gauge, historian_connector_wal_drop_total counter, historian_kafka_batch_size_points histogram) and DataQualityProcessor (historian_dqp_range_violations_total, historian_dqp_stale_values_total, historian_influx_write_latency_ms histogram). Grafana dashboards: Kafka consumer lag per partition (SLA alert >10K), InfluxDB write throughput vs 8K target, OpcUaConnector WAL size and poll lag, data quality event rate by tag and type. PagerDuty P1: OpcUaConnector WAL drop rate >0 (historian gaps occurring), InfluxDB write error rate >0.1%. PagerDuty P2: Kafka consumer lag >10K messages, OpcUaConnector poll lag p95 >500ms, InfluxDB series cardinality >50K.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Infrastructure is all on-premises CAPEX hardware already provisioned. Operational cost: GitLab CI on self-hosted runners, Harbor on existing registry server, MinIO on HDD nodes already provisioned. Estimated power: 9 Kubernetes nodes ~3kW total. Scaling to additional OPC-UA sources: OpcUaConnector scales horizontally with additional replicas and Kafka partition increase; InfluxDB horizontal scaling requires paid enterprise InfluxDB Clustered or migration to InfluxDB 3.0 when available. Current 8,000-tag capacity has 2x headroom on NVMe write IOPS budget.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): OpcUaConnector for 2,000 priority process tags, Kafka ingestion, DataQualityProcessor with range validation, InfluxDB historian-hot bucket, Grafana trend dashboards for process engineers, staging load test gate, Flux CD GitOps, Keycloak auth. Accuracy validation against legacy PI historian for 2-week parallel run before cutover. Phase 2 Full Tag Rollout (8 weeks): Remaining 6,000 tags, warm tier downsampling tasks, MinIO cold Parquet export, QueryAPI REST for batch conformance, quality analyst dashboards, SQLite WAL replay for OpcUaConnector back-pressure. Phase 3 Advanced Analytics (roadmap): Alarm setpoint historian, MES batch record integration, self-service Grafana dashboard builder, InfluxDB 3.0 migration assessment.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go code: all exported functions have godoc comments; error wrapping with fmt.Errorf('context: %w', err); no panic in production code paths. OpcUaConnector unit tests use gopcua/opcua testserver with fixture tag subscriptions. DataQualityProcessor integration tests use testcontainers-go for embedded Kafka and InfluxDB. InfluxDB Flux queries stored in queries/ directory as .flux files loaded at startup via embed.FS — not inline Go string literals. Kubernetes manifests in historian-infra repo with kustomize overlays; all YAML validated by kube-score in CI. No manual kubectl applies; all changes via Flux CD GitOps. Sealed-secrets rotation documented in runbooks/secret-rotation.md.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: OPC-UA server version differences across PLC vendors produce node ID format mismatches that cause connector to miss tags silently. Mitigation: per-PLC node ID mapping config in connector ConfigMap; validate subcommand in connector that polls all configured nodes and reports reachability before production enable. Risk: InfluxDB series cardinality explosion if tag_id has unbounded values. Mitigation: tag_id is a controlled vocabulary from the opc_ua_tags registry; cardinality monitored via Prometheus histogram with PagerDuty alert at 50K series. Risk: SQLite WAL file on ephemeral Pod storage lost on node failure. Mitigation: WAL volume mounted on PVC backed by local-path storage on the same node; node failure causes WAL loss but historian gap is bounded by ring buffer 60s. Tradeoff: InfluxDB 2.7 open-source vs InfluxDB Clustered (paid) — open-source 3-node setup chosen to meet budget; clustering available as upgrade path if cardinality or write rate exceeds single-node capacity.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-002 resolved: two-tier back-pressure (480K entry ring buffer + SQLite WAL with 10-min eviction + WalReplayJob) provides historian continuity during Kafka outages up to 10 minutes without data gaps. ISS-003 resolved: 500ms batch window with MaxBatchSize=4,000 points on InfluxDB WriteAPI validated in staging load test (8K streams, 10 min, p99 <200ms gate). ISS-001 was resolved in round 2 with DataQualityProcessor Go Kafka consumer specification. InfluxDB 2.7 confirmed over TimescaleDB for native TSM compression on high-cardinality time-series; Flux query language provides continuous query tasks without external scheduler dependency.

metadata.notes: Round 3 industrial historian infrastructure: resolves OpcUaConnector SQLite WAL back-pressure design and InfluxDB 8K writes/sec load test gate.

project_goal.value: Fullstack energy management system for a multi-site industrial manufacturer aggregating electricity, gas, and compressed air metering data, benchmarking energy consumption per production unit, and surfacing efficiency recommendations to energy managers
project_goal.rationale: ISO 50001 energy management mandate and EU Energy Efficiency Directive reporting obligations drove project initiation

target_users.value: Energy managers reviewing site-level consumption dashboards and recommendations; process engineers querying energy intensity per production run; site EHS coordinators generating regulatory compliance reports
target_users.rationale: Three distinct user personas identified in stakeholder workshops

project_class.rationale: Requires custom React analytics dashboard, FastAPI backend, TimescaleDB time-series, and integration with building automation and ERP systems

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, analytics, batch_jobs, integrations
capabilities.rationale: Analytics dashboard, meter ingestion, ERP integration, weekly recommendation batch job, Google SSO auth

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-site meter ingestion, TimescaleDB hypertable queries, ERP production volume normalization, regression-based recommendation engine

risk_level.rationale: Internal operational data; no financial or personal data; multi-site access requires RBAC

data_sensitivity.rationale: Energy consumption and production data is internal operational data; not personally identifiable or financial

external_exposure.rationale: Application hosted on GCP with access restricted to corporate VPN; no public internet access

access_model.value: Google Workspace SSO OAuth2 with RBAC: energy_manager (all sites read, recommendation approval), process_engineer (site-scoped read, energy intensity query), site_ehs_coordinator (site-scoped read, report generation), system_admin (user management, meter configuration)
access_model.rationale: Google Workspace is corporate IdP; site-scoped RBAC prevents cross-site data leakage

feature_scope.value: Hourly meter reading ingestion from building automation systems (Modbus TCP, BACnet REST), production volume normalization from ERP via REST, energy intensity (kWh per unit produced) calculation per shift and site, benchmarking dashboard with site comparison, weekly regression-based efficiency recommendation engine, ISO 50001 and EU EED compliance report generator
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature scope approved in energy management steering group

mvp_scope.value: Electricity meter ingestion for 3 pilot sites, energy intensity calculation per shift, site comparison dashboard, production volume import from ERP CSV upload; automated BACnet integration and recommendation engine in phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: CSV ERP import acceptable for MVP; automated integration in phase 2 after API access granted

security_baseline.value: Google Workspace SSO with OAuth2 PKCE flow; TLS 1.3 for all traffic; GCP IAM for GKE workload identity; site-scoped RBAC enforced at API layer; no meter credentials stored in application layer
security_baseline.rationale: Internal application security baseline; meter credentials managed by building automation team

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript, Recharts for energy charts, React Query for server state
frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; Recharts sufficient for time-series area and bar charts; React Query simplifies meter reading refetch patterns

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 with async ORM for TimescaleDB, Celery for background jobs
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI async for meter ingestion throughput; Celery for weekly recommendation batch jobs and ERP sync

data_platform.value: TimescaleDB 2.13 (PostgreSQL 16 extension) for meter readings hypertable; Redis 7 for job queues and dashboard cache
data_platform.rationale: TimescaleDB hypertable time-series compression and time-bucket aggregation SQL functions are ideal for per-shift energy intensity queries

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot in europe-west1 for application workloads; Cloud SQL for TimescaleDB; Memorystore Redis
hosting_target.rationale: GKE Autopilot reduces cluster management overhead; Cloud SQL provides managed HA for TimescaleDB; europe-west1 for EU data residency

privacy_retention_policy.value: Meter readings retained 5 years in TimescaleDB hot tier; energy intensity aggregates retained 10 years; compliance reports retained 10 years in Cloud Storage; data older than 5 years chunked to TimescaleDB compressed tier
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: EU Energy Efficiency Directive requires 10-year audit trail for energy reporting

future_scope.value: BACnet and Modbus TCP automated meter ingestion adapters, real-time energy anomaly alerting, carbon footprint calculation module, SAP PM integration for energy cost allocation to cost centers
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 and 3 roadmap items after MVP validation

constraints.value: EU data residency (europe-west1 only); budget under 2,500 EUR/month GCP OpEx; must integrate with SAP ERP via REST API for production volume data in phase 2; MVP must support 3 sites and 50 electricity meters
constraints.rationale: EU data sovereignty, budget cap, and ERP integration requirements

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for backend service health and Celery job metrics; structured JSON logging to GCP Cloud Logging; PagerDuty for meter ingestion failure rate >5% or recommendation job failure
observability_baseline.rationale: GCP Cloud Logging for log aggregation; Prometheus/Grafana for operational metrics; PagerDuty for on-call

execution_preference.value: Agile 2-week sprints with GitHub Actions CI and GKE Autopilot rolling deployments
execution_preference.rationale: Team workflow preference; GKE Autopilot rolling deploy supports zero-downtime releases

project.goal: Multi-site industrial energy management with meter ingestion, energy intensity benchmarking, and efficiency recommendations
project.target_users: Energy managers, process engineers, site EHS coordinators

frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for UI state
frontend.charting: Recharts for time-series and bar charts

backend.framework: FastAPI with SQLAlchemy 2.0 async

security.auth: Google Workspace SSO OAuth2 PKCE

data.cache: Memorystore Redis 7
data.formats: Modbus/BACnet meter data, SAP ERP REST, CSV upload

open_questions.erp_integration: SAP REST API access timeline for phase 2

reasoner_reviews.product: The energy intensity metric (kWh per unit produced) is the core value driver — it normalizes raw meter consumption against production output and enables cross-site benchmarking. The production volume ERP integration is the critical dependency; CSV upload for MVP is pragmatic. The weekly regression-based recommendation engine must produce site-specific recommendations (e.g., 'compressed air pressure at Site B is 12% above optimal at current throughput level') not generic energy saving tips.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: TimescaleDB time-bucket SQL functions are the right choice for per-shift energy intensity aggregation queries; a regular PostgreSQL table would require application-layer windowing. The Celery recommendation job must be idempotent — it will run weekly and must handle re-runs without duplicating recommendations. FastAPI async is important for the meter ingestion path which will handle burst writes from multiple sites concurrently.
reasoner_reviews.security: Google Workspace SSO with OAuth2 PKCE is appropriate for internal application. Site-scoped RBAC must be enforced at the FastAPI dependency layer, not just in SQL WHERE clauses, to prevent data leakage if a query is mistakenly constructed. GCP workload identity for service account authentication to Cloud SQL and Memorystore avoids storing credentials in environment variables.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 2,500 EUR/month GCP budget constrains GKE Autopilot node pool size. Cloud SQL for TimescaleDB (db-standard-4 with 100GB SSD) costs approximately 400 EUR/month; GKE Autopilot at typical workload approximately 600 EUR/month; Memorystore Redis approximately 100 EUR/month — leaving approximately 1,400 EUR/month headroom. Staying in europe-west1 single region is required for EU data residency; no multi-region Cloud SQL needed for internal app.

backend.service_design: Three FastAPI routers: /meters (meter registry CRUD, ingestion endpoints), /energy (energy intensity queries, site benchmark), /recommendations (weekly recommendation results, approval actions). Celery workers: MeterIngestionWorker (hourly scheduled task polling building automation REST APIs), RecommendationEngine (weekly regression analysis, produces recommendation_actions records).
backend.api_patterns: REST API with OpenAPI 3.0. POST /meters/{id}/readings (ingest batch of meter readings). GET /energy/intensity?site_id=&from=&to=&resolution=shift|day|week (TimescaleDB time-bucket query returning kWh/unit). GET /energy/benchmark?from=&to= (cross-site energy intensity comparison). GET /recommendations?site_id=&status=pending|approved. PATCH /recommendations/{id}/approve.
backend.background_jobs: MeterIngestionWorker: Celery beat task running hourly; fetches meter readings from building automation REST endpoints per site; upserts into meter_readings hypertable. RecommendationEngine: Celery beat weekly Sunday 02:00 UTC; pulls 90-day energy intensity and production volume time series per site; runs linear regression to identify best-efficiency operating conditions; generates recommendation_actions records with estimated_savings_kwh and recommended_parameter.
backend.failure_handling: MeterIngestionWorker: retry 3 times with exponential backoff on building automation API failure; write ingestion_errors record if all retries fail. RecommendationEngine: idempotent via upsert on (site_id, recommendation_date) — safe to re-run. TimescaleDB connection pool exhaustion: SQLAlchemy async pool_size=20 max_overflow=10.

frontend.app_structure: React SPA with route-based code splitting. Routes: /dashboard (multi-site energy intensity overview), /sites/:id (single-site trend and benchmark detail), /recommendations (pending and approved recommendations with savings estimates), /reports (ISO 50001 and EU EED report generation), /admin (meter configuration and user management).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Dashboard: site cards with current-shift energy intensity vs target, traffic-light status (green <100%, amber 100-110%, red >110% of baseline). Site Detail: Recharts AreaChart of energy intensity over time with ERP production volume overlay, comparison against best-performing site. Recommendations: data table with site, recommendation text, estimated_savings_kwh, status; energy_manager can approve with one click.
frontend.state_management: React Query for all server state with 5-minute stale time for dashboard; 30-second for live site view. Zustand for site filter selection and date range picker state shared across pages.
frontend.performance_notes: Dashboard initial load: React Query parallel prefetch of all site current-shift summaries. Recharts lazy-loaded via dynamic import. TimescaleDB queries for 90-day trend served from Redis cache with 15-minute TTL to avoid repeated heavy aggregations.

security.auth_design: Google Workspace SSO OAuth2 PKCE flow. FastAPI dependency site_access_guard(site_id) validates JWT claim 'site_ids' against requested site_id; raises 403 if mismatch. Energy_manager role has site_ids=['*'] for all-site access.
security.authorization_model: RBAC claims in Google Workspace directory groups mapped to roles at JWT validation. Per-site data isolation enforced by FastAPI dependency layer; no reliance on SQL WHERE clause alone.
security.secrets_management: GCP workload identity for FastAPI pods: no Cloud SQL or Memorystore credentials in env vars. Google OAuth2 client secret in GCP Secret Manager mounted as env via GKE workload identity. Building automation API keys stored in GCP Secret Manager per site.
security.security_risks: Building automation API keys exposed if Secret Manager access is misconfigured. Mitigated by GCP IAM binding limiting Secret Manager access to meter-ingestion service account only. Cross-site data leakage mitigated by FastAPI dependency site_access_guard on all /energy and /recommendations routes.

data.entities: energy_meters (meter_id UUID PK, site_id FK, meter_type ENUM electricity/gas/compressed_air, location_description, building_automation_endpoint, active BOOL). meter_readings (meter_id FK, reading_ts TIMESTAMPTZ, consumption_kwh FLOAT, quality_flag BOOL) as TimescaleDB hypertable partitioned by reading_ts with chunk_time_interval 1 day. production_runs (run_id UUID PK, site_id FK, product_code, start_ts, end_ts, units_produced INT, source ENUM erp_sync/csv_upload). energy_intensities (site_id FK, period_start TIMESTAMPTZ, period_end TIMESTAMPTZ, resolution ENUM shift/day/week, total_kwh FLOAT, units_produced INT, intensity_kwh_per_unit FLOAT) as materialized view refreshed hourly. recommendation_actions (id UUID PK, site_id FK, recommendation_date DATE, recommendation_text, estimated_savings_kwh FLOAT, parameter_name, recommended_value, status ENUM pending/approved/dismissed, approved_by, created_at).
data.storage_design: TimescaleDB Cloud SQL (db-standard-4, 100GB SSD). meter_readings hypertable with 1-day chunk intervals; compression enabled after 7 days (lossless delta-delta encoding for float readings). Redis Memorystore: site benchmark cache (key: benchmark:{from}:{to}, TTL 15min), dashboard summary cache (key: dashboard:{site_id}:{shift_date}, TTL 5min).
data.analytics_events: energy_intensity_viewed (site_id, resolution, date_range), recommendation_approved (site_id, recommendation_id, estimated_savings_kwh), report_generated (site_id, report_type, period).

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster in europe-west1. Workloads: api (FastAPI, 2-4 replicas, HPA on CPU 60%), worker (Celery, 2 replicas for ingestion and recommendation), frontend (Nginx serving React SPA, 2 replicas). Cloud SQL: db-standard-4 PostgreSQL 16 with TimescaleDB extension in europe-west1-b. Memorystore Redis 7 STANDARD_HA in europe-west1.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: on PR, run ruff, mypy, pytest with testcontainers TimescaleDB and Redis fixtures (80% coverage gate), React TypeScript tsc and vitest. On merge to main, build Docker images and push to Artifact Registry, deploy to staging GKE via kubectl rollout. Production deploy on semver tag with team approval gate.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping FastAPI /metrics (meter ingestion rate, recommendation job duration, API latency histogram) and Celery flower metrics. Grafana dashboards: meter ingestion success rate per site, energy intensity API p95 latency, Celery job queue depth. GCP Cloud Logging for structured JSON logs. PagerDuty: meter ingestion failure rate >5% on any site, recommendation job failure.
devops.cost_controls: GKE Autopilot: scale-to-zero for worker deployment outside scheduled job windows. Cloud SQL: 1 read replica only when reporting load requires. Committed use discount for Cloud SQL baseline. Budget alert at 2,200 EUR/month.

target_output.thinking_summary: Energy management system for multi-site manufacturer: TimescaleDB hypertable for meter readings with time-bucket aggregation for per-shift energy intensity queries is the core architectural decision. Site-scoped RBAC enforced at FastAPI dependency layer is critical for multi-site data isolation. Weekly Celery regression-based recommendation engine must be idempotent. GKE Autopilot with Cloud SQL TimescaleDB in europe-west1 satisfies EU data residency and 2,500 EUR/month budget constraint.
target_output.title: Multi-Site Industrial Energy Management System
target_output.executive_summary: A fullstack application aggregating electricity, gas, and compressed air meter readings across multiple industrial sites, normalizing consumption against ERP production volumes to compute per-shift energy intensity (kWh per unit produced), benchmarking sites against each other, and running a weekly regression-based recommendation engine that identifies efficiency improvement opportunities. Built on React 18, FastAPI, and TimescaleDB on GKE Autopilot in europe-west1 for EU data residency. ISO 50001 and EU Energy Efficiency Directive compliance reports generated on demand by site EHS coordinators.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture with React SPA served via GKE Nginx ingress communicating to a FastAPI backend over HTTPS within corporate VPN. FastAPI connects to TimescaleDB on Cloud SQL for time-series meter data, Memorystore Redis for dashboard caching, and GCP Secret Manager for building automation API keys. Two Celery workers: MeterIngestionWorker (hourly, polls BAS REST endpoints per site) and RecommendationEngine (weekly, regression analysis over 90-day energy intensity data). Site-scoped RBAC enforced via FastAPI site_access_guard dependency on all /energy and /recommendations routes. Google Workspace SSO OAuth2 PKCE for authentication. All data hosted in europe-west1 for EU data residency compliance.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18 with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Recharts for energy charts, React Query, Zustand. Backend: Python 3.12 with FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Celery 5.3 with Redis broker. Database: TimescaleDB 2.13 on Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (db-standard-4, 100GB SSD). Cache: Memorystore Redis 7 STANDARD_HA. Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot europe-west1, GCP Artifact Registry, Cloud SQL, Memorystore, Secret Manager, Cloud Logging. Auth: Google Workspace OAuth2 with python-jose JWT validation. CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Monitoring: Prometheus + Grafana, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: MeterIngestionWorker: hourly Celery beat task fetching consumption readings from building automation REST endpoints; upserts into meter_readings hypertable with quality flag validation. ERP Production Volume Import: CSV upload endpoint (MVP) and SAP REST sync (phase 2) writing to production_runs table. Energy Intensity Engine: TimescaleDB time-bucket SQL computing kWh/unit per shift, day, and week; results materialized as energy_intensities view refreshed hourly. Site Benchmark Dashboard: React dashboard showing all-site energy intensity comparison with traffic-light status vs baseline targets. RecommendationEngine: weekly Celery regression analysis identifying top-3 efficiency improvement opportunities per site with estimated savings in kWh and EUR. ISO 50001 and EU EED Report Generator: PDF report generation from energy_intensities aggregates covering user-specified date range.
target_output.system_components: GKE Autopilot Workloads: api (FastAPI, 2-4 replicas, HPA CPU 60%), worker-ingestion (Celery MeterIngestionWorker, 2 replicas), worker-recommendations (Celery RecommendationEngine, 1 replica, scheduled), frontend (Nginx React SPA, 2 replicas). Cloud SQL: TimescaleDB hypertable for meter_readings, materialized view energy_intensities, tables energy_meters, production_runs, recommendation_actions, site_configurations. Memorystore Redis 7: Celery broker queues, dashboard cache, benchmark cache. GCP Secret Manager: building automation API keys per site, Google OAuth2 client secret, Cloud SQL connection string. Cloud Logging: structured JSON logs from all services. GCP Artifact Registry: Docker image storage. GitHub Actions: CI/CD pipeline.
target_output.workflows: Meter Ingestion Workflow: MeterIngestionWorker fires hourly via Celery beat; for each active energy_meter record with building_automation_endpoint, calls site BAS REST API with site-specific API key from Secret Manager; parses response into (meter_id, reading_ts, consumption_kwh, quality_flag) tuples; batch upserts into meter_readings TimescaleDB hypertable using ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE; logs ingestion_result (success/partial/failed) per meter to structured log. On failure after 3 retries, writes to ingestion_errors table and triggers PagerDuty if site failure rate >5%. Energy Intensity Query Workflow: Process engineer opens Site Detail page; React Query fetches GET /energy/intensity?site_id=B&from=2026-01-01&to=2026-03-24&resolution=shift; FastAPI site_access_guard validates JWT site_ids claim includes 'B'; queries energy_intensities materialized view with time-bucket aggregation; result cached in Redis with key intensity:B:2026-01-01:2026-03-24:shift TTL 15min; Recharts renders area chart with ERP production volume overlay.
target_output.data_model: energy_meters (meter_id UUID PK, site_id UUID FK, meter_type ENUM electricity/gas/compressed_air, location_description VARCHAR, building_automation_endpoint VARCHAR, polling_interval_minutes INT DEFAULT 60, active BOOL). meter_readings TimescaleDB hypertable (meter_id UUID FK, reading_ts TIMESTAMPTZ, consumption_kwh FLOAT NOT NULL, quality_flag BOOL DEFAULT true, ingested_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()) partitioned by reading_ts 1-day chunks; compression after 7 days. production_runs (run_id UUID PK, site_id UUID FK, product_code VARCHAR, start_ts TIMESTAMPTZ, end_ts TIMESTAMPTZ, units_produced INT, source ENUM erp_sync/csv_upload, imported_at). energy_intensities materialized view: SELECT site_id, time_bucket('1 shift', reading_ts) as period, SUM(consumption_kwh) as total_kwh, SUM(units_produced) as units, SUM(consumption_kwh)/NULLIF(SUM(units_produced),0) as intensity_kwh_per_unit FROM meter_readings JOIN production_runs USING (site_id) GROUP BY site_id, period. recommendation_actions (id UUID PK, site_id UUID FK, recommendation_date DATE, recommendation_text TEXT, estimated_savings_kwh FLOAT, parameter_name VARCHAR, recommended_value VARCHAR, status ENUM pending/approved/dismissed, approved_by UUID, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). utility_tariffs (tariff_id UUID PK, site_id UUID FK, energy_type ENUM electricity/gas/compressed_air, rate_eur_per_kwh FLOAT, effective_from DATE, effective_to DATE).
target_output.api_design: FastAPI REST at /api/v1 with OpenAPI 3.0. Auth: Bearer JWT from Google OAuth2 validated via python-jose. Site guard: site_access_guard(site_id) dependency on all site-scoped endpoints. Endpoints: POST /auth/callback (OAuth2 PKCE code exchange). GET /sites (list sites with current energy intensity summary, role-filtered). GET /energy/intensity?site_id=&from=&to=&resolution=shift|day|week (time-bucket energy intensity series). GET /energy/benchmark?from=&to= (cross-site intensity comparison, energy_manager only). POST /meters/{id}/readings/upload (CSV bulk upload for MVP). GET /recommendations?site_id=&status= (list recommendations). PATCH /recommendations/{id}/approve (energy_manager only). POST /reports/generate?site_id=&type=iso50001|eu_eed&from=&to= (triggers async PDF generation). GET /reports/{id} (download PDF from Cloud Storage).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Google Workspace SSO OAuth2 PKCE: state parameter validated server-side to prevent CSRF; JWT validated via python-jose with Google JWKS endpoint. Site-scoped RBAC: site_access_guard FastAPI dependency checks JWT 'site_ids' claim against requested site_id; energy_manager role receives site_ids=['*']. GCP workload identity for all GKE pods: no Cloud SQL password or Memorystore AUTH token in environment variables; authenticated via service account binding. Building automation API keys in GCP Secret Manager; mounted as env var only in worker-ingestion deployment. TLS 1.3 for all external and internal GKE traffic via GCP-managed certificates. ISO 50001 audit trail: energy_intensities and recommendation_actions records are append-only (no soft delete); report generation events logged to Cloud Logging.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot europe-west1 with Workload Identity enabled. GitHub Actions CI: ruff lint, mypy --strict, pytest with testcontainers-python providing TimescaleDB and Redis fixtures (80% coverage gate), React TypeScript tsc --noEmit, vitest for component tests. Docker images pushed to Artifact Registry on main merge. Staging deploy via kubectl rollout restart on staging GKE namespace. Production deploy on semver tag with team lead approval via GitHub Environment protection rule. Rolling deploy strategy: maxSurge=1, maxUnavailable=0 for zero-downtime releases. Database migrations via Alembic with CI migration dry-run check. Celery beat scheduler runs in worker-recommendations deployment with CronJob-style Redis lock to prevent concurrent execution.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics via prometheus-fastapi-instrumentator: http_request_duration_seconds histogram, meter_ingestion_success_total and meter_ingestion_failure_total counters per site_id label, celery_task_duration_seconds histogram. Grafana dashboards: meter ingestion success rate per site (SLA alert <95%), energy intensity API p95 latency (alert >2s), Celery recommendation job duration and failure count, Cloud SQL connection pool utilization. GCP Cloud Logging: structured JSON with correlation_id, site_id, user_id on every API request. PagerDuty: meter ingestion failure rate >5% for any site, RecommendationEngine Celery task failure, Cloud SQL CPU >80%.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated GCP OpEx: Cloud SQL db-standard-4 ~400 EUR/month, GKE Autopilot idle baseline ~300 EUR/month (scale-to-zero for workers outside scheduled windows), Memorystore Redis Basic ~80 EUR/month, Artifact Registry and Cloud Logging ~50 EUR/month. Total ~830 EUR/month at MVP scale, well within 2,500 EUR/month cap. Scaling: TimescaleDB compression keeps meter_readings disk growth at ~5GB/year for 50 meters at hourly resolution; Cloud SQL can scale to db-standard-8 if query latency degrades. GKE Autopilot auto-scales API replicas on CPU. Phase 2 with 200 meters and 10 sites estimated at ~1,400 EUR/month.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): TimescaleDB schema and meter_readings hypertable, MeterIngestionWorker for electricity meters at 3 pilot sites, CSV production volume upload, energy intensity calculation with time-bucket SQL, site comparison dashboard with Recharts, Google SSO auth with site RBAC, GKE Autopilot deployment, GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2 (8 weeks): Gas and compressed air meter types, BACnet and Modbus TCP adapter plugins for automated ingestion, SAP ERP REST sync for production volume, RecommendationEngine weekly regression analysis, recommendation approval workflow. Phase 3 (6 weeks): ISO 50001 PDF report generator, EU EED compliance export, carbon footprint module with emission factor lookup, cost allocation integration with SAP PM cost centers.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python: mypy strict, ruff lint. All FastAPI route handlers use async def with SQLAlchemy async session. TimescaleDB queries written as SQLAlchemy Core text() with named parameters — no ORM for hypertable queries to control time-bucket function usage. Celery tasks are idempotent by design: MeterIngestionWorker uses ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE; RecommendationEngine upserts on (site_id, recommendation_date). All API endpoints have pytest integration tests using testcontainers TimescaleDB fixture with seed meter_readings data. React: TypeScript strict mode; Recharts charts have unit tests asserting data transformation functions. No site_id values in frontend URL state without site_access_guard backend validation.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Building automation REST API format variability across sites requires per-site adapter configuration. Mitigation: site_configurations table with adapter_type and field_mapping JSON; adapter plugin pattern in MeterIngestionWorker. Risk: energy_intensities materialized view refresh lag means dashboard can show 1-hour stale data. Mitigation: refresh triggered by MeterIngestionWorker on successful ingestion completion; TTL cache makes lag visible to users as 'last updated' timestamp. Tradeoff: TimescaleDB continuous aggregate vs hourly refresh materialized view — materialized view chosen for simplicity and compatibility with Cloud SQL managed PostgreSQL; continuous aggregate in future if sub-minute query latency required. Tradeoff: CSV ERP upload vs automated SAP REST sync — CSV chosen for MVP to unblock deployment while SAP API access is provisioned.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected TimescaleDB over InfluxDB for energy data because time-bucket SQL aggregation joins naturally with relational production_runs table for energy intensity calculation; InfluxDB would require application-layer join. Chose Celery with Redis broker over Cloud Tasks for recommendation engine because Celery beat provides familiar Python-native scheduling with existing Redis infrastructure. CSV ERP upload for MVP confirmed acceptable by ERP team; SAP API access on Phase 2 timeline. europe-west1 single region confirmed sufficient for EU data residency; multi-region not required for internal application.

metadata.notes: First-pass multi-site industrial energy management system: TimescaleDB energy intensity hypertable, Celery recommendation engine, site-scoped RBAC, GKE Autopilot europe-west1.

frontend.interactivity: React islands for ROI calculator and lead form

backend.framework: None

security.auth: None
security.transport: HTTPS via CloudFront, HSTS preload, CSP headers

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with Lighthouse CI gate

reasoner_reviews.product: The ROI calculator is the primary conversion driver — welding and palletizing cell calculators cover the two highest-volume application segments. Case study gallery filterable by industry (automotive, food & beverage, logistics) addresses varied visitor personas. Lead form qualification fields (number of shifts, annual production volume, timeline to invest) will improve sales team efficiency.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro island architecture is the correct choice: the hero, case studies, and partner logo carousel are fully static with zero runtime JS, while the ROI calculator island uses React for reactive state. This split keeps transferred JS well under the 150 KB budget. Video embeds should use facade pattern with lazy loading to avoid third-party script weight on initial load.
reasoner_reviews.security: CSP policy must block inline scripts except from the hCaptcha and GA4 domains. HSTS preload with 1-year max-age. CloudFront should return X-Frame-Options: DENY and X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff. Form submissions go directly to HubSpot Forms API over HTTPS from the browser — no server-side proxy means no Lambda cold-start risk.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 150 KB JS budget is achievable with Astro partial hydration: React island for ROI calculator is estimated at 45 KB gzipped, Tailwind purged CSS under 12 KB. Lighthouse CI gate in GitHub Actions will catch regressions before merge. CloudFront edge cache with Cache-Control: max-age=31536000 for hashed assets ensures repeat visitor performance.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Previous rounds failed to address image optimization — case study hero images were uncompressed JPEGs causing LCP violations on mobile. This round must enforce WebP with responsive srcset, width/height attributes, and fetchpriority=high on the above-fold hero. Video facade pattern was specified but not implemented in the build pipeline.

frontend.app_structure: Astro 4 project with src/pages/index.astro as entry, src/components for static Astro components, src/islands for React interactive components (RoiCalculator.tsx, LeadForm.tsx). Content collections for case_studies and page_sections defined via Astro content schema.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Single-page layout with anchor-linked sections: #hero, #case-studies, #roi-calculator, #lead-form, #partners. Navigation sticky header with smooth-scroll. ROI calculator section switches between welding-cell and palletizing-cell tabs with shared state in React island. Lead form displays inline success confirmation on HubSpot submission.
frontend.performance_notes: All images served as WebP via Astro Image component with responsive srcset (320w/640w/1280w). LCP hero image gets fetchpriority=high and preload link tag. Video embeds use lite-youtube-embed web component loaded after user interaction. Lighthouse CI gate runs on every PR with budgets: LCP <2s, TBT <200ms, CLS <0.1.
frontend.frontend_risks: hCaptcha script weight (~85 KB) is a budget risk — load it lazily after form scroll-into-view event. GA4 with consent mode v2 must not fire measurement calls before user cookie consent, requiring careful initialization order in the Astro head component.

security.auth_design: No authentication required. hCaptcha v2 on lead capture form with server-side token verification via HubSpot's native CAPTCHA integration.
security.abuse_prevention: hCaptcha on lead form submission. CloudFront rate limiting via AWS WAF managed rules for common bot signatures. No API endpoints to protect.
security.privacy_controls: GDPR cookie consent banner using Cookiebot integrated with GA4 consent mode v2. Analytics events fire only after explicit consent. No personally identifiable data stored on AWS infrastructure.
security.security_risks: Dependency supply chain risk from CDN-hosted third-party scripts (hCaptcha, GA4). Mitigation: pin script hashes in CSP and run automated dependency audits via npm audit in CI.

data.entities: case_studies (slug, title, industry_tag, robot_type, throughput_improvement_pct, payback_months, hero_image_url, body_mdx). roi_calculator_inputs (client-side only, not persisted: cell_type, parts_per_hour, rejects_rate_pct, labor_cost_usd, annual_production_days). lead_captures (forwarded to HubSpot, not stored: company, name, email, phone, shift_count, volume_units_per_year, invest_timeline).
data.analytics_events: page_view, case_study_filter_applied (industry_tag), roi_calculator_result_viewed (cell_type, payback_months), lead_form_started, lead_form_submitted (HubSpot contact_id returned), video_facade_clicked (video_id).
data.data_risks: GA4 data residency: EU visitor data routed to EU storage via GA4 data region setting. No PII transmitted to analytics — form field values are not included in GA4 events.

devops.deployment_topology: GitHub Actions builds Astro site on push to main. Output dist/ synced to S3 bucket with versioned object keys. CloudFront distribution invalidated for index.html and sitemap.xml on deploy. PR preview deployments use S3 key prefix per PR number with a separate CloudFront behavior.
devops.ci_cd_design: Pipeline stages: install deps (bun install), type-check (tsc --noEmit), lint (eslint), build (astro build), lighthouse-ci (lhci autorun against built site), s3-deploy (aws s3 sync dist/ s3://bucket). Lighthouse CI configured with minimum scores: performance 90, accessibility 95, best-practices 90, SEO 90.
devops.cost_controls: S3 + CloudFront estimated $8/month at 500K monthly page views. CloudFront caching policy max-age 31536000 for hashed assets, 3600 for index.html. No Lambda functions or compute costs.
devops.devops_risks: CloudFront invalidation latency after deploy can cause brief mixed-content state if index.html references new asset hashes. Mitigation: use content-addressed file names (Astro default) so old and new assets coexist during propagation.

backend.service_design: No backend service. Lead form submits directly to HubSpot Forms API v2 endpoint from the browser using fetch().
backend.api_patterns: POST https://api.hsforms.com/submissions/v3/integration/submit/:portalId/:formId with JSON body containing lead fields. Response handling in LeadForm.tsx React island shows success/error state.
backend.backend_risks: HubSpot API availability is a single point of failure for lead capture. Mitigation: on HubSpot API error, store submission in localStorage and retry on next page load, and display fallback email address to the user.

ISS-001.title: LCP violation on mobile due to uncompressed case study images
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 audit measured LCP of 4.2s on Moto G4 profile in Lighthouse CI due to 1.8 MB uncompressed JPEG hero images in case study cards. WebP conversion with responsive srcset was specified but not implemented.

ISS-002.title: Video embeds blocking main thread on initial load
ISS-002.detail: YouTube iframe embeds were loading full player scripts on page load, adding 380 KB of third-party JS and increasing TBT to 620ms. Facade pattern using lite-youtube-embed was recommended but not implemented until round 3.

ISS-003.title: GA4 firing analytics events before cookie consent
ISS-003.detail: GA4 gtag initialized unconditionally in <head>, transmitting measurement data for EU visitors before consent dialog acknowledgment, violating GDPR consent requirement. Consent mode v2 initialization order was not specified.

focus_issues.detail: Round 1 audit measured LCP of 4.2s on Moto G4 profile in Lighthouse CI due to 1.8 MB uncompressed JPEG hero images in case study cards. WebP conversion with responsive srcset was specified but not implemented.

target_output.title: Industrial Robotics Integrator Landing Page — Final Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A high-performance static marketing site for an industrial robotics integrator, built with Astro 4 and deployed to AWS S3+CloudFront. React islands power the welding-cell and palletizing-cell ROI calculators; the case study gallery, hero, and partner carousel are zero-JS static HTML. Lead captures submit directly to HubSpot Forms API v2 from the browser with hCaptcha bot protection. All three prior performance and compliance issues are resolved: WebP images with responsive srcset and fetchpriority=high achieve LCP under 2s on mobile, the lite-youtube-embed facade eliminates third-party video script weight from initial load, and GA4 consent mode v2 fires analytics only after explicit cookie consent.
target_output.architecture_overview: Fully static architecture: Astro 4 SSG generates HTML at build time for all page sections (hero, case study gallery, partner logos, video section) with zero runtime JavaScript. Interactive components — the ROI calculator tabs and lead capture form — are hydrated as React 18 islands using client:visible directive, so they load only when scrolled into view. The built dist/ directory is deployed to an S3 bucket configured as an origin for a CloudFront distribution with Origin Access Control. CloudFront serves all traffic globally from edge PoPs, with Cache-Control: max-age=31536000 for Astro-hashed asset files and max-age=3600 for index.html. No Lambda@Edge, no server-side compute. Form submissions flow from the browser directly to HubSpot Forms API v2 over HTTPS.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Astro 4.x with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS 3, React 18 (islands), lite-youtube-embed web component, hCaptcha v2 client library. Analytics: Google Analytics 4 with Cookiebot consent mode v2 integration. Build tools: Bun as package manager, Astro CLI, tsc, ESLint, Stylelint. CI: GitHub Actions with Lighthouse CI (lhci). Hosting: AWS S3 (versioned bucket), CloudFront with OAC, Route 53, ACM. Monitoring: CloudWatch synthetic canaries, CloudFront access logs.
target_output.functional_feature_map: ROI Calculator (React island): welding-cell tab (inputs: parts/hour, rejects %, labor cost, annual production days; outputs: payback months, 5-year NPV) and palletizing-cell tab (inputs: pallets/hour, stack patterns, shift count; outputs: cycle time reduction, ROI %). Case Study Gallery (static Astro): 6 filterable entries by industry tag (automotive, food-and-beverage, logistics, general-manufacturing); each card shows robot type, throughput improvement %, and payback months. Lead Capture Form (React island): fields for company, name, email, phone, shift count, annual production volume, investment timeline; submits to HubSpot Forms API v2. Video Section (static Astro + lite-youtube-embed): 3 robot demo reels loaded on user interaction. Partner Logo Carousel (static Astro CSS animation): 8 integration partner logos.
target_output.system_components: S3 Bucket (versioned, private, OAC policy) serving as CloudFront origin. CloudFront Distribution (OAC, custom cache behaviors, HTTP→HTTPS redirect, custom error pages for 404). Route 53 A-record alias pointing to CloudFront. ACM certificate in us-east-1 for CloudFront HTTPS. GitHub Actions runner executing build and deploy pipeline. CloudWatch Synthetic Canary (Node.js script) polling index.html every 5 minutes for HTTP 200 and LCP metric. HubSpot Forms API v2 receiving lead submissions directly from browser. GA4 property with Cookiebot consent mode v2 tag configuration.
target_output.workflows: ROI Calculator Interaction: Visitor scrolls to calculator section triggering client:visible hydration of RoiCalculator.tsx React island. User selects cell type tab (welding or palletizing), enters production parameters into controlled inputs, and the component computes payback period and NPV client-side using configurable labor and capital cost formulas — no network request required. Results update reactively. A 'Request Detailed Analysis' CTA pre-fills the lead form with the entered parameters. Lead Capture Submission: Visitor completes lead form fields; on submit, LeadForm.tsx island calls hCaptcha.execute() to obtain a token, then POSTs to HubSpot Forms API v2 with lead fields and hCaptcha token. HubSpot validates the CAPTCHA server-side. On success the island renders an inline confirmation. On API error the island stores the payload in localStorage under pending_lead_submission and displays the fallback sales email address.
target_output.data_model: case_studies (Astro content collection, Zod schema): slug string, title string, industry_tag enum(automotive|food_and_beverage|logistics|general_manufacturing), robot_type string, application string, throughput_improvement_pct number, payback_months number, hero_image_src string (WebP), body_mdx string. roi_calculator_inputs (client-side React state only, never persisted): cell_type enum(welding|palletizing), parts_per_hour number, rejects_rate_pct number, labor_cost_usd_per_hour number, annual_production_days number, capital_cost_usd number. lead_captures (submitted to HubSpot, not stored on AWS): company string, contact_name string, email string, phone string, shift_count number, volume_units_per_year number, invest_timeline_months number, captcha_token string.
target_output.api_design: No server-side API endpoints. External API calls from browser: POST https://api.hsforms.com/submissions/v3/integration/submit/{portalId}/{formGuid} with body {fields:[{name:'email',value:'...'}, ...], context:{pageName:'robotics-integrator-landing'}, legalConsentOptions:{consent:{consentToProcess:true,text:'...',communications:[{value:true,subscriptionTypeId:999,text:'...'}]}}} and response handling for 200 OK (show success) and 4xx/5xx (show error and store to localStorage). No REST API is exposed by this project.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced: CloudFront HTTP→HTTPS redirect, HSTS header Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload. CSP header: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://js.hcaptcha.com https://www.googletagmanager.com 'sha256-{astro-inline-hash}'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' data: https:; connect-src 'self' https://api.hsforms.com https://www.google-analytics.com. X-Frame-Options: DENY. X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff. Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin. GDPR: Cookiebot consent mode v2 integrated; GA4 measurement calls suppressed until visitor grants analytics consent; no PII included in GA4 events; cookie consent banner displayed to all visitors, pre-checked only for strictly necessary cookies.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: CI/CD Pipeline (GitHub Actions): Trigger on push to main and on PR open/update. Steps: (1) bun install --frozen-lockfile, (2) tsc --noEmit, (3) eslint src/, (4) astro build, (5) lhci autorun --config=lighthouserc.json (blocks merge if performance <90, accessibility <95, SEO <90), (6) aws s3 sync dist/ s3://$BUCKET --delete --cache-control 'max-age=31536000,immutable' --exclude 'index.html' --exclude 'sitemap.xml', (7) aws s3 cp dist/index.html s3://$BUCKET/index.html --cache-control 'max-age=3600', (8) aws cloudfront create-invalidation --distribution-id $CF_DIST --paths '/index.html' '/sitemap.xml'. PR Preview: PRs deploy to s3://$BUCKET/preview/pr-{number}/ and are served via a CloudFront behavior with path pattern /preview/pr-* routing to the same S3 origin. Preview URL posted as GitHub PR comment by the workflow. Production promotion: merge to main triggers full deploy with invalidation.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch Synthetic Canary (Node.js blueprint) runs every 5 minutes against production URL, asserting HTTP 200 and capturing LCP metric via PerformanceObserver; alarms if LCP >3000ms or canary failure. CloudFront access logs streamed to CloudWatch Logs Insights for error rate (4xx, 5xx) and cache hit ratio queries. GA4 conversion funnel: roi_calculator_result_viewed → lead_form_started → lead_form_submitted tracked as a GA4 conversion event with HubSpot contact_id as event parameter for lead attribution. Lighthouse CI run results stored as GitHub Actions artifacts for trend comparison across PRs.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $8/month at 500K monthly page views: S3 storage $0.50, CloudFront data transfer ~$6, CloudWatch canary ~$0.50, Route 53 $0.50. Zero compute costs — no Lambda, no EC2. CloudFront scales to millions of requests without configuration changes. Cache hit ratio target >95% for hashed assets, reducing S3 origin requests to near zero.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (completed — 3 weeks): Astro project scaffold, Tailwind design tokens from brand guide, hero section, 6 case study cards with WebP images, ROI calculator React island (welding and palletizing tabs), lead form with hCaptcha and HubSpot integration, GA4 with Cookiebot consent mode v2, GitHub Actions pipeline with Lighthouse CI gate, S3+CloudFront deploy. Phase 2 (2 weeks): Video section with lite-youtube-embed facade, partner logo carousel, PR preview deployment workflow. Phase 3 (2 weeks): Localized versions for DACH market (German copy in Astro i18n routing), A/B testing framework using CloudFront Functions for variant assignment.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode in Astro and React islands. ESLint with @typescript-eslint and eslint-plugin-astro. Stylelint for Tailwind class ordering. Lighthouse CI budget file (lighthouserc.json) committed to repo and reviewed on every PR. All images must be processed through Astro Image component — no raw tags with external src. hCaptcha site key and HubSpot portal/form IDs stored as GitHub Actions secrets and injected at build time as Astro environment variables.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: HubSpot Forms API unavailability causes silent lead loss. Mitigation: localStorage fallback stores failed submissions and retries on next page visit; fallback email address displayed on API error. Risk: Lighthouse CI gate blocks hotfix deploys when performance budget is tight after a content change. Mitigation: emergency bypass label on PR allows CI skip with mandatory post-deploy audit. Tradeoff: no server-side lead validation means HubSpot must handle deduplication — acceptable for a marketing page at this scale. Tradeoff: Astro content collections require a build step for case study updates — trade-off versus a CMS is acceptable for the current 6-entry catalog, but a Netlify CMS or Keystatic integration should be evaluated when the catalog exceeds 20 entries.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected hCaptcha over reCAPTCHA v3 to avoid Google data processing for EU visitors. Confirmed lite-youtube-embed over YouTube iframe API to eliminate 380 KB third-party script from initial load. Confirmed HubSpot Forms API v2 direct browser submission over a Lambda proxy to eliminate compute costs. Cache-Control strategy: max-age=31536000,immutable for hashed Astro assets, max-age=3600 for index.html.

metadata.notes: Round 4 convergence plan for static robotics integrator landing page; all prior issues resolved, PR preview workflow and cache headers fully specified.

project_goal.value: Migrate 15 years of production and quality records from a legacy Oracle-based MES into a modern cloud data lakehouse, normalizing schema inconsistencies, enriching with master data from ERP, and making historical data queryable for analytics
project_goal.rationale: Legacy Oracle MES is being decommissioned; historical traceability data must be preserved and made queryable for quality audits and process improvement analytics

target_users.value: Process engineers running root-cause analyses, quality managers pulling traceability reports for customer audits, data analysts building production performance dashboards
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers of migrated historical data identified in stakeholder sessions

project_class.rationale: Pure ETL/ELT pipeline; no frontend or API service required

capabilities.rationale: Batch extraction from Oracle, transformation in Spark, loading to Delta Lake, with ERP integration for master data enrichment

complexity_level.rationale: 15-year schema evolution across multiple Oracle versions, ERP join complexity, and automotive traceability retention requirements drive advanced complexity

risk_level.rationale: Confidential production data, regulatory retention requirements, but no real-time operations risk — pipeline failure does not halt production

data_sensitivity.rationale: Production records, quality data, and customer part traceability are competitively sensitive and subject to IATF audit

external_exposure.rationale: Delta Lake data accessible only to authenticated internal users via Databricks workspace and internal BI tools

access_model.value: IAM role-based access to S3 Delta Lake; Databricks workspace RBAC for catalog, schema, and table-level permissions by role (analyst, engineer, auditor)
access_model.rationale: Principle of least privilege; auditors access quality tables only, analysts access aggregated views

feature_scope.value: Full historical migration of production_orders, operation_records, quality_inspections, material_lots, equipment_events; ERP enrichment for material master and customer order linkage; data quality reporting dashboard; incremental sync for ongoing MES delta until decommission
feature_scope.rationale: Complete migration scope required for decommission sign-off

mvp_scope.value: Bronze-layer ingestion of all 5 Oracle source tables, basic schema normalization, silver-layer enrichment with ERP material master, data quality dashboard showing row counts and null rates per table
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP needed for initial quality audit validation by Q3

security_baseline.value: S3 bucket encryption with SSE-KMS, IAM roles with no wildcard permissions, VPC endpoint for S3 and EMR, no Oracle credentials in code — stored in AWS Secrets Manager, Databricks workspace in customer-managed VPC
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential data requires encryption at rest and in transit, with no credential exposure in pipeline code

frontend_stack.value: None — pipeline only; Databricks SQL and Grafana for analytics access
frontend_stack.rationale: No custom frontend; analysts use Databricks SQL Editor and Grafana dashboards against Delta Lake

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Apache Spark on EMR Serverless; PySpark jobs orchestrated by Apache Airflow on MWAA
backend_stack.rationale: Spark for scalable distributed transformation; MWAA for managed Airflow with AWS native integration

data_platform.value: Delta Lake on AWS S3 with three-tier medallion architecture (bronze, silver, gold); Unity Catalog on Databricks for data governance
data_platform.rationale: Delta Lake provides ACID transactions and time-travel for audit compliance; Unity Catalog enforces table-level permissions

hosting_target.value: AWS EMR Serverless for Spark jobs, MWAA for Airflow orchestration, S3 for Delta Lake storage, Databricks workspace in AWS for querying
hosting_target.rationale: Serverless EMR eliminates cluster management overhead; MWAA provides managed Airflow without self-hosting

privacy_retention_policy.value: IATF 16949 requires 10-year minimum retention for automotive part production records; Delta Lake time-travel snapshots retained for 365 days; raw Oracle extracts in S3 bronze retained for 12 months post-migration then archived to Glacier
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Automotive customer contractual retention requirements

future_scope.value: Gold-layer aggregations for Databricks SQL dashboards, real-time streaming pipeline from new MES post-decommission, ML feature store for predictive quality models
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 analytics and ML enablement roadmap

constraints.value: Oracle source must remain read-only during migration; full migration must complete within 6-month window before decommission; EMR Serverless budget under $3000/month during migration phase
constraints.rationale: Decommission schedule and budget limits drive timeline and cost constraints

observability_baseline.value: Airflow task-level logs in CloudWatch, Spark job metrics in EMR console, data quality metrics published to CloudWatch custom namespace, Grafana dashboard for migration progress
observability_baseline.rationale: Migration monitoring must cover both pipeline health and data quality

execution_preference.value: Iterative medallion tier delivery: bronze in sprint 1, silver in sprint 2-3, gold in sprint 4; incremental jobs deployed after full historical load completes
execution_preference.rationale: Phased delivery enables early validation by analysts before migration completes

compliance_context.value: IATF 16949 part production record traceability retention (10-year minimum); audit trail of every schema transformation step required for quality audit defense
compliance_context.rationale: Automotive customer contracts and IATF certification require demonstrable data lineage

project.goal: Migrate 15 years of Oracle MES records to Delta Lake on S3 with schema normalization and ERP enrichment
project.target_users: Process engineers, quality managers, data analysts

frontend.approach: Databricks SQL Editor and Grafana for data access

backend.framework: Apache Spark on EMR Serverless, Airflow on MWAA

security.auth: IAM roles, Databricks RBAC
security.secrets: AWS Secrets Manager for Oracle credentials

data.cache: None

devops.environments: dev (sample data), staging (1 year slice), production (full 15 years)

open_questions.erp_access: JDBC or file export from SAP S/4HANA for material master data

reasoner_reviews.product: The three-tier medallion architecture is the correct approach for this migration scope. Bronze captures raw Oracle extracts with full fidelity, silver applies normalization and ERP enrichment, gold produces analytics-ready aggregations. This staged approach allows quality validation at each tier before promotion, which is essential for IATF audit defensibility.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Oracle JDBC extraction via Spark should use partition columns on order_date or operation_date to parallelize reads across the 15-year dataset. Estimated Oracle source size of 800 GB to 1.2 TB requires careful partition sizing — recommend 200 MB per Spark partition, approximately 4000-6000 partitions for the production_orders table. Delta Lake write with mergeSchema enabled for bronze to accommodate schema drift across Oracle version upgrades in the historical data.
reasoner_reviews.security: Oracle source credentials must be retrieved from Secrets Manager at job startup — never hardcoded or logged. S3 bucket policy should deny PutObject without SSE-KMS header to prevent unencrypted writes. EMR Serverless execution role should be scoped to specific S3 prefixes (bronze/, silver/, gold/) with no wildcard actions. Databricks workspace must be deployed in a customer-managed VPC with no public IP on cluster nodes.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Six-month migration window is achievable with EMR Serverless parallelism. Full historical load of 15 years estimated at 3-4 weeks for bronze, 2 weeks for silver enrichment, 1 week for gold aggregations. Incremental sync using CDC timestamp queries runs nightly thereafter until decommission. $3000/month EMR budget is sufficient for the historical load phase using 4-vCPU executor configuration on EMR Serverless.

backend.service_design: Three PySpark job families: (1) extract_oracle — partitioned JDBC reads per source table writing to bronze S3 prefix as Delta, (2) transform_silver — dedup, schema normalization, ERP join writing to silver Delta tables, (3) aggregate_gold — time-window aggregations per plant/product/shift for gold Delta tables. Airflow DAGs orchestrate job sequencing with dependency checks.
backend.api_patterns: No external API. Inter-job interfaces use Delta Lake table state: downstream jobs read from upstream Delta table with a quality_gate_passed=true partition filter, ensuring only validated bronze data enters silver processing.
backend.background_jobs: Historical backfill DAG runs once: bootstrap bronze per table, then silver, then gold. Incremental DAG runs nightly: CDC extract for rows modified since last high-watermark, upsert to bronze via Delta MERGE, trigger silver refresh for affected partitions.
backend.failure_handling: Airflow tasks configured with retries=3, retry_delay=10 min. Spark job failure writes failure record to migration_checkpoints Delta table with job_name, partition_range, error_message, failed_at. Dead-letter S3 prefix for rows that fail schema coercion after 3 retries — reviewed daily by pipeline operator.
backend.backend_risks: Oracle JDBC driver version compatibility with Spark 3.5 must be validated in dev before production run. ERP SAP S/4HANA JDBC access may be blocked by SAP licensing; fallback is daily CSV export from SAP to S3.

security.auth_design: EMR Serverless job roles assume distinct IAM roles per pipeline tier (emr-bronze-role, emr-silver-role, emr-gold-role) with S3 write permissions scoped to their target prefix only. Cross-tier reads use S3 bucket policy conditions on role ARN.
security.secrets_management: Oracle and SAP JDBC credentials stored in Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation. EMR Serverless job retrieves secrets via boto3 at runtime. Secret ARNs passed as Spark config properties, not environment variables, to avoid CloudWatch log exposure.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every Spark job writes a transformation_audit record to a dedicated Delta table: job_id, source_table, rows_extracted, rows_loaded, rows_rejected, schema_version, executed_at, emr_job_run_id. This lineage table is the IATF audit artifact demonstrating data transformation history.
security.security_risks: Spark executor logs may contain sample data rows on error. Mitigation: configure log4j to suppress data-level logging and use structured logging with row counts only.

data.entities: production_orders (id, plant_code, part_number, quantity_ordered, quantity_completed, scheduled_start, actual_start, actual_end, work_center_id, status, source_mes_id). operation_records (id, production_order_id, operation_number, work_center_id, setup_time_min, run_time_min, operator_badge, completed_at, source_mes_id). quality_inspections (id, production_order_id, inspection_type ENUM final/in-process/receiving, result ENUM pass/fail/conditional, inspector_badge, measured_dimensions_json, defect_codes_array, inspected_at). material_lots (id, material_number, lot_number, quantity, unit_of_measure, received_date, expiry_date, supplier_id, certificate_number). equipment_events (id, equipment_id, event_type ENUM planned_downtime/unplanned_downtime/maintenance/calibration, start_time, end_time, reason_code, technician_badge). erp_materials (material_number, description, material_group, base_unit, customer_part_number, drawing_revision). migration_checkpoints (job_name, table_name, high_watermark_ts, rows_processed, status, executed_at).
data.storage_design: S3 bucket with three prefix tiers: bronze/ (raw Oracle extracts, Delta format, partitioned by plant_code/source_date), silver/ (normalized and ERP-enriched, partitioned by plant_code/year/month), gold/ (aggregated metrics, partitioned by plant_code/year). Delta Lake with OPTIMIZE and ZORDER on frequently queried columns (part_number, production_order_id, inspected_at).
data.retention_and_deletion: Bronze tables retained 12 months post-migration then transitioned to S3 Glacier via lifecycle rule. Silver and gold tables retained 10 years per IATF 16949. Delta time-travel log retention 365 days. Deletion requests handled via Delta GDPR delete command on specific lot or order IDs if customer requests data removal.
data.data_risks: Schema drift in 15-year Oracle source — column renames, type changes across version upgrades. Mitigation: bronze uses mergeSchema=true and a schema_version column populated from Oracle db_version. Deduplication risk: Oracle MES had known duplicate operation_records from a 2019 ETL bug — handled by ROW_NUMBER() dedup in silver transform keyed on source_mes_id.

devops.deployment_topology: GitHub Actions deploys PySpark job scripts to S3 jobs/ prefix on merge to main. Airflow DAG files deployed to MWAA S3 DAG bucket. EMR Serverless application pre-provisioned with warm capacity for nightly incremental runs. Databricks Unity Catalog schemas created via Terraform. Environments: dev (20 GB Oracle sample, single-node EMR), staging (1 year slice, 100 GB), production (full 15 years, auto-scaling EMR Serverless up to 200 vCPUs).
devops.ci_cd_design: Pipeline: pytest for PySpark unit tests using PySpark local mode (no cluster required), flake8 + black lint, job script upload to S3, Airflow DAG validation via airflow dags test. Staging environment runs a smoke migration of 1 year of data before production promotion.
devops.observability_stack: Airflow task logs in CloudWatch Logs. EMR Spark metrics (executor utilization, shuffle bytes, GC time) in EMR console and exported to CloudWatch. Custom CloudWatch metrics: rows_extracted_total, rows_rejected_total, silver_promotion_lag_hours. Grafana dashboard with migration progress panels: tables completed, row counts by tier, daily rejection rate trend.
devops.devops_risks: MWAA version upgrades require DAG compatibility testing — pin Airflow provider versions in requirements.txt. EMR Serverless cold start latency (60-90s) may be unacceptable for nightly incremental jobs; mitigate with pre-initialized capacity configuration.

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a round 1 migration data pipeline for 15 years of Oracle MES data into a Delta Lake medallion architecture on AWS.
