previous_plan.title: IoT Telemetry Platform Cost Reduction - Lambda Migration Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Migrates EC2 telemetry processors to Lambda triggered by Kinesis, deploys S3 lifecycle tiering, and implements idle resource termination to reduce monthly AWS cost from $12,000 to ~$7,200.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Python 3.12 Lambda functions replace EC2 normalizer and aggregator, triggered by Kinesis Data Streams event source mappings. S3 Intelligent-Tiering and Glacier lifecycle applied to historian bucket. Terraform manages all IaC.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: Terraform modules for lambda-processors, s3-historian. GitHub Actions for plan/apply. Parallel-run validation before EC2 termination. Migration window Sunday 02:00-06:00 UTC.
previous_plan.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: data loss during cutover. Mitigation: parallel run. Risk: Lambda errors. Mitigation: DLQ. Cold-start risk acknowledged but not mitigated.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 addresses ISS-001 (Lambda cold-start) with Application Auto Scaling provisioned concurrency on a shift-start schedule, and ISS-002 (NAT Gateway costs) with S3 Gateway VPC Endpoint and DynamoDB Interface VPC Endpoint added to Terraform. The DLQ and bisect-on-error gaps from round 1 audit are also incorporated. Core parallel-run migration strategy is unchanged and sound.

fix_report.action_taken: Added Application Auto Scaling provisioned concurrency schedule to TelemetryNormalizerLambda: 2 reserved instances warmed from 05:30 UTC Monday-Saturday (30 min before shift start), scaled back to 0 at 21:00 UTC. Provisioned instances absorb the first post-cold-start invocation burst. On-demand concurrency handles mid-shift scale-out. Cold-start benchmark target documented: provisioned <200ms, on-demand <1200ms with container image optimization.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Kinesis iterator age remains <60 seconds at shift start; no back-pressure alarms triggered during shift ramp-up period.

fix_report.action_taken: Added aws_vpc_endpoint for S3 (Gateway type, free) and DynamoDB (Interface type, ~$22/month) to lambda-processors Terraform module. All Lambda traffic to DynamoDB and S3 now routes via VPC endpoints, eliminating NAT Gateway data processing charges estimated at $800/month. Net saving from VPC endpoints: $778/month after endpoint cost.
fix_report.expected_outcome: NAT Gateway data processing charges for Lambda eliminated; monthly saving increases from $4,800 to $5,578.

target_output.title: IoT Telemetry Platform Cost Reduction - Lambda Migration with Cold-Start Mitigation
target_output.executive_summary: An AWS infrastructure migration for a plastics manufacturer that replaces always-on EC2 telemetry processors with event-driven Python Lambda functions triggered by Kinesis Data Streams, implementing provisioned concurrency schedules for shift-start cold-start mitigation, VPC endpoints for cost-efficient DynamoDB and S3 access, and S3 Intelligent-Tiering with Glacier lifecycle for historian data. Targets a reduction from $12,000/month to ~$6,500/month.
target_output.architecture_overview: Kinesis Data Streams receive IoT telemetry from injection molding and extrusion machines. TelemetryNormalizerLambda (Python 3.12 container image) is triggered by Kinesis event source mapping with batch size 100 and parallelization factor 1 per shard. Application Auto Scaling warms 2 provisioned concurrency instances at 05:30 UTC Monday-Saturday before shift start, scales to zero at 21:00 UTC. TelemetryAggregatorLambda is triggered by DynamoDB Streams to compute 5-minute rolling averages and write summary records to S3. Both Lambda functions run in private VPC subnets with S3 Gateway VPC Endpoint and DynamoDB Interface VPC Endpoint eliminating NAT Gateway charges. S3 historian bucket uses Intelligent-Tiering for objects <90 days and transitions to Glacier Instant Retrieval at 90 days via lifecycle policy. CloudWatch cost anomaly detection alerts if Lambda invocation cost exceeds $200/month.
target_output.technology_stack: Compute: Python 3.12 on AWS Lambda (container images from ECR). Event streaming: Kinesis Data Streams (existing). State: DynamoDB (telemetry_partitions, processor_state). Storage: S3 Intelligent-Tiering + Glacier Instant Retrieval lifecycle. IaC: Terraform 1.8 with modular layout. CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Observability: CloudWatch Lambda metrics, Kinesis iterator age alarms, CloudWatch cost anomaly detection, Grafana (existing) consuming CloudWatch metrics via datasource.
target_output.functional_feature_map: TelemetryNormalizerLambda: deserializes Kinesis records (JSON telemetry payloads from Modbus and OPC-UA gateways), applies machine_id schema mapping, writes normalized records to S3 partitioned by machine_id/YYYY/MM/DD/HH/, updates telemetry_partitions DynamoDB record. TelemetryAggregatorLambda: triggered by DynamoDB Streams INSERT events on telemetry_partitions, reads raw S3 partition, computes per-machine 5-minute averages for pressure, temperature, and cycle time, writes aggregated JSON to S3 aggregates/ prefix. Idle EC2 Detector: EventBridge daily rule triggers Python Lambda that queries CloudWatch EC2 CPU metrics; instances with <2% avg CPU for 7 days are tagged for-termination and a Slack notification sent to platform team for manual approval before termination. Cost Report Lambda: runs 1st of month, queries Cost Explorer API, writes monthly spend breakdown by service to S3, posts summary to Slack #platform-costs channel.
target_output.system_components: Kinesis Data Streams (4 shards, 24-hour retention during migration, 7-day post-migration), TelemetryNormalizerLambda (container image, 512MB memory, 60-second timeout, 2 provisioned concurrency instances 05:30-21:00 weekdays), TelemetryAggregatorLambda (container image, 256MB memory, 30-second timeout), DynamoDB tables (telemetry_partitions, processor_state, on-demand billing), S3 historian bucket (Intelligent-Tiering + Glacier lifecycle, S3 Gateway VPC Endpoint), DynamoDB Interface VPC Endpoint (eu-west-1), Lambda VPC (private subnets in 3 AZs), SQS DLQs per Lambda function, CloudWatch alarms (iterator age, DLQ depth, cost anomaly), EventBridge rules (daily idle EC2 check, monthly cost report), Application Auto Scaling target for provisioned concurrency.
target_output.workflows: Shift-Start Warm-Up: At 05:30 UTC, Application Auto Scaling publishes a scheduled action to set provisioned_concurrency_config to 2 for TelemetryNormalizerLambda; Lambda pre-initializes 2 container instances. At 06:00 UTC, first shift machines come online, Kinesis receives telemetry; provisioned instances handle burst without cold start, iterator age stays <30 seconds. Migration Cutover: During Sunday 02:00 UTC window, Terraform applies disable EC2 normalizer cron job (scales ASG to 0), enables Lambda event source mapping for TelemetryNormalizerLambda as primary consumer; Kinesis 24-hour retention ensures replay window. Monitors Kinesis iterator age for 30 minutes; if iterator age >5 minutes, rollback by re-enabling EC2 ASG and disabling Lambda ESM - achievable in <5 minutes via Terraform targeted apply. Post-Cutover Validation: GitHub Actions integration test sends 10,000 synthetic Kinesis records and validates corresponding DynamoDB records are written within 2 minutes; validates S3 objects are created with correct partitioning.
target_output.data_model: DynamoDB telemetry_partitions: PK machine_id#date (String), SK hour (String), s3_path (String), record_count (Number), size_bytes (Number), storage_class (String ENUM intelligent_tiering/glacier_instant), created_at (Number epoch), TTL (Number epoch 3-year expiry). DynamoDB processor_state: PK function_name (String), last_sequence_number (String), last_processed_at (Number epoch), error_count (Number), provisioned_warm (Boolean). S3 key structure: s3://telemetry-historian-prod/{machine_id}/{YYYY}/{MM}/{DD}/{HH}/{uuid}.json.gz. S3 aggregates: s3://telemetry-historian-prod/aggregates/{machine_id}/{YYYY}/{MM}/{DD}/{metric_name}.json.
target_output.api_design: No REST API exposed. Internal event contracts: Kinesis record payload schema: {machine_id: string, timestamp_ms: number, measurements: [{tag: string, value: number, unit: string}], protocol: string}. DynamoDB Streams event triggers TelemetryAggregatorLambda; NEW_IMAGE projected. EventBridge rule schema for idle EC2 check: {source: 'aws.events', detail-type: 'Scheduled Event', resources: ['arn:aws:events:eu-west-1:...:rule/idle-ec2-detector-daily']}. Cost Explorer API call: get_cost_and_usage with granularity MONTHLY, groupBy SERVICE, filter by tag Environment=production.
target_output.security_and_compliance: IAM least-privilege: TelemetryNormalizerLambda execution role scoped to kinesis:GetRecords on specific stream ARN, s3:PutObject on historian bucket prefix telemetry/*, dynamodb:PutItem/UpdateItem on telemetry_partitions and processor_state table ARNs. No s3:* or dynamodb:* wildcards. Lambda runs in private subnets eu-west-1a/1b/1c. S3 Gateway VPC Endpoint (com.amazonaws.eu-west-1.s3) with endpoint policy restricting access to historian bucket. DynamoDB Interface VPC Endpoint (com.amazonaws.eu-west-1.dynamodb). S3 bucket: block all public access, server-side encryption with SSE-S3. CloudTrail enabled for all Lambda invocations and S3 object writes. Kinesis stream encrypted with AWS managed KMS key.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform 1.8 modules: lambda-processors (Lambda functions, IAM roles, event source mappings, Application Auto Scaling, DLQs), vpc-endpoints (S3 Gateway, DynamoDB Interface endpoints), s3-historian (bucket, lifecycle rules, server-side encryption), kinesis-config (stream shard count, retention period update). State backend: S3 + DynamoDB lock in existing Terraform backend account. GitHub Actions pipeline: PR - terraform fmt check, terraform validate, tflint, checkov IaC scan, pytest Lambda unit tests. Merge to main - terraform plan saved as artifact, manual approval, terraform apply to staging, integration test (10K synthetic Kinesis records), 30-minute soak, manual approval, terraform apply to production. Migration cutover run as separate GitHub Actions workflow with migration-specific steps: extend Kinesis retention to 24 hours, enable Lambda ESM, monitor iterator age, confirmation step before EC2 ASG scale-down.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch alarms: KinesisIteratorAgeTooHigh (iterator age >5 min for 2 consecutive 1-min periods, SNS -> PagerDuty), LambdaDLQDepth (DLQ messages > 0, SNS -> Slack #platform-alerts), LambdaErrorRate (errors/invocations > 1% over 5 minutes, SNS -> PagerDuty). CloudWatch cost anomaly detection monitor on Lambda service with 10% threshold alert to #platform-costs. Grafana dashboard updated: Lambda invocation count, duration p99, Kinesis iterator age, S3 Intelligent-Tiering vs Glacier object count distribution. Monthly cost report Lambda writes JSON to s3://platform-reports/cost/{YYYY-MM}.json and posts formatted summary to Slack.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Expected monthly cost post-migration: Lambda compute ~$350 (at 8M invocations/month), Lambda provisioned concurrency ~$120 (2 instances x 15.5 hours/weekday x 22 days), Kinesis ~$400, DynamoDB on-demand ~$200, S3 storage ~$800 (Intelligent-Tiering saves 40% vs Standard), S3 Glacier for cold data ~$150, DynamoDB VPC endpoint ~$22, total ~$2,042 vs current $12,000. Target 40% reduction achieved; actual projected reduction is ~83% on compute alone but blended with storage savings reaches 83%. Provisioned concurrency adds $120/month vs ~$500/month NAT Gateway eliminated by VPC endpoints - net benefit clear.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (weeks 1-2): Terraform modules scaffolded, Lambda container images built from existing EC2 Python code, unit tests, staging deployment, integration test with synthetic Kinesis data. Phase 2 (weeks 3-4): VPC endpoints deployed to production, S3 lifecycle policies applied to historian bucket, cost baseline captured in CloudWatch. Phase 3 (weeks 5-6): TelemetryNormalizerLambda parallel-run as shadow consumer alongside EC2; validator Lambda compares outputs; iterate on discrepancies. Phase 4 (week 7): Production cutover during Sunday 02:00 UTC migration window; EC2 ASG scaled to 0 after 30-minute validation; Kinesis retention remains 24 hours for 48 hours post-cutover. Phase 5 (week 8): TelemetryAggregatorLambda migration; idle EC2 detector deployed; remaining EC2 instances terminated; cost dashboard published.
target_output.development_guidelines: Terraform: all resources tagged with Environment, Service, CostCenter. No hardcoded ARNs - use data sources and locals. checkov scan in CI with CRITICAL findings blocking merge. Lambda Python: no mutable global state between invocations; use POWERTOOLS_SERVICE_NAME env var for AWS Lambda Powertools structured logging. Unit tests mock boto3 clients with moto. Integration tests use separate staging Kinesis stream to avoid production data contamination.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Lambda container image cold start exceeds provisioned concurrency capacity during unexpected traffic spikes. Mitigation: CloudWatch alarm on concurrency utilization > 80% of provisioned capacity triggers auto-scaling review. Risk: Terraform state conflict during migration window if multiple engineers apply simultaneously. Mitigation: DynamoDB state lock enforced; migration pipeline requires exclusive lock via a GitHub Actions environment mutex. Tradeoff: provisioned concurrency adds $120/month vs eliminating cold-start risk for shift-start bursts - acceptable given 4800+ monthly saving. Tradeoff: DynamoDB Interface VPC Endpoint costs $22/month vs eliminating NAT Gateway charges - clearly net positive.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Cold-start mitigation: Application Auto Scaling provisioned concurrency schedule (05:30 UTC Mon-Sat). NAT Gateway avoidance: S3 Gateway + DynamoDB Interface VPC endpoints. Kinesis retention during migration: 24 hours. Rollback mechanism: disable Lambda ESM, re-enable EC2 ASG via Terraform targeted apply, achievable in <5 minutes.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision addressing Lambda cold-start and NAT Gateway cost issues for IoT telemetry platform migration.

profile.domain: Manufacturing Research - CNC Cutting Data Correlation Prototype

project_goal.value: Aggregate multi-sensor cutting data (spindle load, feed force, acoustic emission, chip morphology images) from a CNC turning cell to discover correlations between cutting parameter combinations and tool wear rate, enabling researchers to design systematic follow-on experiments.
project_goal.rationale: Manual data collection across four sensor types into separate spreadsheets prevents cross-sensor correlation analysis

target_users.value: Manufacturing research engineers and PhD researchers at university-affiliated machining laboratory
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified in lab kickoff meeting

project_class.rationale: Exploratory research tool; not a production system; emphasis on analytical flexibility over uptime

capabilities.rationale: FastAPI for data ingestion and query, MongoDB for multi-modal data, Jupyter for correlation analysis, sensor integration adapters

complexity_level.rationale: Four heterogeneous sensor types with different sampling rates require careful schema design but workflow is straightforward

risk_level.rationale: Research prototype on isolated lab server; no production systems affected

data_sensitivity.rationale: Cutting parameter data is proprietary research but not regulated; JWT authentication sufficient

external_exposure.rationale: Accessible only on lab network; no internet exposure

access_model.value: Custom JWT for API write access; JupyterLab behind lab VPN; no RBAC required for single-team use
access_model.rationale: Small single-team research environment

feature_scope.value: Cutting experiment logging with parameter set capture, synchronized multi-sensor data storage, chip morphology image upload, tool wear measurement logging, correlation analysis notebooks, structured findings export
feature_scope.rationale: Covers full data lifecycle from raw capture to publishable findings

mvp_scope.value: Experiment logging API, spindle load and acoustic emission sensor storage, tool wear measurement capture, basic Jupyter notebooks for data loading and plotting
mvp_scope.rationale: Feed force sensor integration and chip image analysis can follow after core data model is validated

security_baseline.value: Custom JWT for API, HTTPS on lab network, MongoDB authentication enabled, no public access
security_baseline.rationale: Low-risk internal research prototype; proportionate security controls

frontend_stack.value: none - JupyterLab notebooks serve as the analysis interface
frontend_stack.rationale: Researchers prefer Jupyter for exploratory data analysis; no dedicated web frontend required

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI for data ingestion and query API
backend_stack.rationale: Python ecosystem aligns with researcher familiarity and scientific computing libraries

data_platform.value: MongoDB 7 for multi-modal experiment metadata; local filesystem numpy .npy files for high-rate timeseries
data_platform.rationale: MongoDB document model accommodates variable-length sensor channel manifests; filesystem avoids 16MB document limit

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal Linux server in machining laboratory
hosting_target.rationale: Lab server provides direct network access to CNC cell sensor interfaces

privacy_retention_policy.value: All experiment data retained indefinitely for research continuity; no personal data processed
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Research datasets must be preserved for publication reproducibility

future_scope.value: Automated ML-based wear rate prediction model trained on accumulated dataset, integration with CNC machine controller API for automated parameter adjustment experiments
future_scope.rationale: Post-prototype roadmap pending sufficient dataset accumulation

constraints.value: Must run on existing bare-metal Linux server; no cloud budget; Jupyter notebooks must be usable by researchers without MongoDB async query knowledge
constraints.rationale: Lab has fixed hardware budget and diverse researcher skill levels

observability_baseline.value: Grafana and Prometheus for API health metrics; structured JSON logging from FastAPI; disk usage monitoring via node_exporter
observability_baseline.rationale: Basic observability for diagnosing data ingestion issues during experiments

execution_preference.value: Iterative development with researchers validating data models after each experiment batch; GitLab CI for automated testing
execution_preference.rationale: Research-driven iteration cycles rather than fixed sprints

project.goal: Multi-sensor CNC cutting data aggregation and correlation analysis for tool wear research
project.target_users: Manufacturing research engineers and PhD researchers
project.timeline: MVP in 6 weeks; feed force and chip image integration in weeks 7-10

backend.framework: FastAPI with Motor 3 async MongoDB driver
backend.notebooks: JupyterLab 4 with pandas, numpy, scipy, matplotlib, seaborn

data.storage: Local filesystem numpy .npy files for sensor timeseries
data.schema: Separate sensor_channels collection referencing experiment_id with file_path field

constraints.audience: researchers with Python familiarity but not software engineers

reasoner_reviews.product: The correlation analysis goal is well-served by MongoDB document storage for experiment metadata. Round 1 plan correctly deferred feed force and chip image integration. The key research value is the structured findings export enabling comparison across experiments and a simple data access library for notebook users.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Round 1 plan stored sensor timeseries as flat arrays embedded in MongoDB documents. At 10kHz acoustic emission sampling for a 5-minute cut, this produces arrays approaching the MongoDB 16MB document size limit. Sensor timeseries must be stored as numpy .npy files on the local filesystem with only metadata references in the sensor_channels MongoDB collection.
reasoner_reviews.security: Custom JWT with HTTPS on lab network is proportionate. MongoDB auth must be enabled and the FastAPI service must use a dedicated MongoDB user with readWrite access scoped to the lab_cutting_db database only.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Researchers will load data in Jupyter notebooks. The round 1 plan expected researchers to write Motor async queries directly. A synchronous lab_client helper package is required: ExperimentClient.list(), SensorLoader.load(experiment_id, channel_type) returning numpy array, WearLoader.to_dataframe(experiment_id) returning pandas DataFrame.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with routers: experiments (CRUD), sensor-data (ingest and retrieve timeseries), wear-measurements (tool wear logging), findings (structured correlation findings). Motor async MongoDB driver. Background task for post-ingest data quality checks.
backend.api_patterns: REST with OpenAPI 3.0 auto-generated by FastAPI. JWT bearer token required for write endpoints; GET endpoints accessible on lab network without token for notebook convenience.
backend.failure_handling: Pydantic validation rejects malformed sensor payloads at ingestion. Duplicate experiment IDs return 409. SensorIngestService writes .npy file then inserts MongoDB sensor_channels record atomically - if MongoDB insert fails, .npy file is deleted and 500 returned.
backend.backend_risks: Orphaned .npy files if ingest crashes after file write but before MongoDB insert. Mitigation: nightly cleanup cron deletes .npy files with no corresponding sensor_channels MongoDB record.

data.entities: cutting_experiments: experiment_id UUID, tool_id, workpiece_material, cutting_params (feed_rate_mmrev, depth_of_cut_mm, spindle_speed_rpm, coolant enum dry/flood/mql), start_ts, end_ts, status enum running/complete/aborted, notes. sensor_channels: experiment_id ref, channel_type enum spindle_load/acoustic_emission/feed_force/chip_image, sample_rate_hz, record_count, file_path, quality_flag enum ok/clipped/noisy/missing. tool_wear_measurements: experiment_id ref, measurement_number, flank_wear_vb_mm, crater_wear_kt_mm, measured_at, image_path. correlation_findings: finding_id UUID, experiment_ids array, hypothesis, statistical_method, r_value, p_value, conclusion, exported_at.
data.storage_design: MongoDB for experiment metadata and findings. Sensor timeseries stored as numpy .npy files at /data/sensor-files/{experiment_id}/{channel_type}.npy. Chip morphology images at /data/chip-images/{experiment_id}/{measurement_number}.jpg. JupyterLab container has read-only mount of /data/sensor-files for direct numpy loading.
data.analytics_events: lab_client Python package: ExperimentClient(api_url, token).list(material, tool_id, date_from), ExperimentClient.get(experiment_id), SensorLoader(api_url, token).load(experiment_id, channel_type) returns numpy ndarray, WearLoader(api_url, token).to_dataframe(experiment_id) returns pandas DataFrame.

devops.deployment_topology: docker-compose on Ubuntu 22.04: fastapi-api uvicorn port 8000, mongodb port 27017 with auth and persistent volume, jupyterlab port 8888 with lab_client pre-installed, prometheus port 9090, grafana port 3000, node_exporter for disk metrics.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: MR gate runs pytest with mongomock fixtures, ruff lint, mypy type checking. Main merge triggers SSH deploy to lab server running docker-compose pull api and docker-compose up --no-deps api.
devops.devops_risks: Lab server disk space finite. Prometheus node_exporter alert when /data volume usage exceeds 80% of allocated capacity.

ISS-001.title: High-rate sensor timeseries embedded in MongoDB documents will exceed 16MB limit
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 data model stores sensor timeseries as inline float arrays in the cutting_experiments MongoDB document. At 10kHz acoustic emission sampling for a 5-minute cut this produces approximately 12MB of float data, approaching the MongoDB 16MB document limit. Writes will throw document too large errors mid-experiment.

history.note: Identified by architect_reasoner review

ISS-002.title: Jupyter notebook data access requires MongoDB async query syntax knowledge
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan expects researchers to write Motor async queries in notebooks to load experiment data. Most researchers lack async Python and MongoDB syntax experience. Without a simple synchronous helper library, notebook adoption will be poor and researchers will revert to spreadsheets.

history.note: Identified by constraints review

focus_issues.title: High-rate sensor timeseries embedded in MongoDB documents will exceed 16MB limit
focus_issues.detail: 10kHz acoustic emission timeseries as inline arrays in experiment documents will cause write failures for long cuts.

focus_issues.title: Jupyter notebook data access requires MongoDB async query syntax
focus_issues.detail: No helper library defined; researchers must write raw Motor async queries.

latest_recommendations: Store sensor timeseries as numpy .npy files on local filesystem with path references in sensor_channels MongoDB collection
Implement lab_client Python package with synchronous helpers installable via pip
Update data_model section to show separate sensor_channels collection with file_path field
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: CNC Tool Wear Correlation Research Prototype - Round 1

convergence_state.reason: High-severity document size issue unresolved after round 1

previous_audits.summary: Good research prototype structure with appropriate MongoDB choice for variable schema, but the document size design flaw for high-rate timeseries is a critical correctness issue that would cause experiment data loss.
strengths: MongoDB document model appropriate for heterogeneous cutting parameter schemas
FastAPI OpenAPI auto-generation simplifies API exploration for researchers
docker-compose deployment is pragmatic for lab server
concerns: Inline timeseries storage will hit MongoDB 16MB document limit
No researcher-friendly data access abstraction planned
Disk space monitoring not addressed
blocking_issues: 10kHz acoustic emission timeseries at 5-minute cuts produces ~12MB of floats - inline storage will fail
recommendations: Move timeseries to numpy .npy files with MongoDB storing file_path metadata only
Create lab_client pip package with sync helper methods for notebook data loading
Add Prometheus node_exporter disk usage alert at 80% threshold

issue_updates.title: MongoDB 16MB document limit for timeseries
issue_updates.detail: Data model shows sensor_data List[float] embedded in experiment document

issue_updates.title: No Jupyter helper library
issue_updates.detail: Notebooks section shows raw Motor queries without abstraction

previous_plan.title: CNC Tool Wear Correlation Research Prototype - Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: FastAPI backend with MongoDB 7 storing cutting experiments with inline sensor timeseries and tool wear measurements. JupyterLab notebooks for correlation analysis on bare-metal lab server.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: FastAPI on uvicorn behind lab network. MongoDB 7 stores all experiment data including sensor timeseries as embedded arrays. docker-compose orchestrates services.
previous_plan.data_model: cutting_experiments document with embedded sensor_data containing spindle_load and acoustic_emission as List[float] arrays. tool_wear_measurements embedded as nested array. Researchers write Motor async queries directly in notebooks.
previous_plan.workflows: Researcher POSTs experiment to API with cutting parameters. Sensor DAQ script POSTs timeseries float arrays to sensor-data endpoint. Researcher opens Jupyter and writes Motor async query to load and analyze data.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 addresses ISS-001 by moving sensor timeseries from inline MongoDB arrays to numpy .npy files on the local filesystem with sensor_channels MongoDB collection storing path references, eliminating the 16MB document limit constraint. ISS-002 is resolved by defining the synchronous lab_client Python package with ExperimentClient, SensorLoader, and WearLoader helpers that abstract Motor async internals from notebook users. No change to FastAPI/MongoDB/JupyterLab technology stack.

fix_report.action_taken: Introduced separate sensor_channels MongoDB collection where each document stores experiment_id reference, channel_type, sample_rate_hz, record_count, and file_path pointing to a numpy .npy file at /data/sensor-files/{experiment_id}/{channel_type}.npy. SensorIngestService writes .npy file first then inserts the sensor_channels MongoDB record; if MongoDB insert fails, .npy file is deleted and 500 returned. 10kHz acoustic emission for a 5-minute cut produces ~24MB .npy file stored on filesystem without any MongoDB document size constraint.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Experiment data ingest completes without MongoDB document size errors for all sensor types and cut durations up to 60 minutes.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined lab_client Python package installable via pip install -e /opt/lab-client, pre-installed in JupyterLab container. Provides synchronous blocking API: ExperimentClient(api_url, token).list(material=None, tool_id=None, date_from=None) returns list of experiment dicts; ExperimentClient.get(experiment_id) returns full document; SensorLoader(api_url, token).load(experiment_id, channel_type) streams .npy from GET /sensor-data/:id/:channel/stream and returns numpy ndarray; WearLoader(api_url, token).to_dataframe(experiment_id) returns pandas DataFrame with measurement_number, flank_wear_vb_mm, crater_wear_kt_mm columns.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Researchers load and analyze experiment data in Jupyter notebooks using 3-5 lines of Python with no MongoDB query syntax knowledge required.

target_output.title: CNC Tool Wear Correlation Research Prototype - Multi-Sensor Data Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A Python FastAPI research prototype on a bare-metal lab server that aggregates spindle load, acoustic emission, feed force, and chip morphology image data from CNC turning experiments. High-rate sensor timeseries are stored as numpy .npy files on the local filesystem with MongoDB 7 holding experiment metadata and sensor channel manifests. Researchers perform correlation analysis between cutting parameters and tool wear rate through JupyterLab 4 notebooks using the synchronous lab_client helper library.
target_output.architecture_overview: FastAPI application on uvicorn handles experiment ingestion and query via REST. Cutting experiment metadata, sensor channel manifests with file_path references, tool wear measurements, and correlation findings are stored in MongoDB 7 with authentication enabled. Sensor timeseries (spindle_load at 1kHz, acoustic_emission at 10kHz, feed_force at 1kHz) are written as numpy .npy files at /data/sensor-files/{experiment_id}/{channel_type}.npy by SensorIngestService, bypassing the MongoDB 16MB document limit. JupyterLab 4 has read-only access to /data/sensor-files via shared docker volume. Researchers access all data through the synchronous lab_client package that wraps FastAPI endpoints and returns numpy arrays and pandas DataFrames directly. docker-compose orchestrates all services on Ubuntu 22.04 bare-metal lab server.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, Motor 3 async MongoDB driver, Pydantic v2. Notebooks: JupyterLab 4, pandas, numpy, scipy, matplotlib, seaborn, scikit-learn. Database: MongoDB 7 auth enabled with dedicated lab_cutting_db user. Timeseries storage: numpy .npy format on local filesystem at /data/sensor-files. Client library: lab_client synchronous requests-based package. Orchestration: docker-compose 2.24. CI: GitLab CI. Observability: Grafana, Prometheus, node_exporter for disk monitoring.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Experiment Management: POST /experiments logs new cutting_experiment with tool_id, workpiece_material, and cutting parameter set; GET /experiments supports filters by material, tool_id, date range. Sensor Data Ingest: POST /sensor-data/{experiment_id}/{channel_type} accepts numpy binary payload, writes .npy file to /data/sensor-files, inserts sensor_channels MongoDB record with file_path. Tool Wear Logging: POST /wear-measurements/{experiment_id} logs flank_wear_vb_mm, crater_wear_kt_mm, and optional image path. Notebook Access: SensorLoader.load(experiment_id, channel_type) streams .npy from API stream endpoint and returns numpy ndarray. Findings Export: POST /findings logs hypothesis, statistical method, p_value, conclusion; GET /findings generates JSON for publication appendix.
target_output.system_components: FastAPI uvicorn server port 8000 JWT required for writes, MongoDB 7 container port 27017 auth enabled with /data/mongodb persistent volume, JupyterLab 4 container port 8888 with lab_client pre-installed and read-only /data/sensor-files mount, /data/sensor-files volume shared between fastapi-api and jupyterlab containers, Prometheus port 9090, Grafana port 3000, Prometheus node_exporter for disk and system metrics, lab_client Python package installable from /opt/lab-client.
target_output.workflows: Cutting Experiment Ingest: Lab DAQ script initiates experiment via POST /experiments receiving experiment_id. Sensor acquisition scripts run during cut and stream spindle_load and acoustic_emission .npy payloads to POST /sensor-data/{id}/spindle_load and /sensor-data/{id}/acoustic_emission concurrently. After cut, technician enters flank wear measurement via POST /wear-measurements/{id}. Notebook Correlation Analysis: Researcher imports lab_client, calls ExperimentClient(url, token).list(material='Ti-6Al-4V') to retrieve relevant experiments. Calls SensorLoader(url, token).load(exp_id, 'acoustic_emission') to get 10kHz numpy array. Joins with WearLoader(url, token).to_dataframe(exp_id) in pandas to correlate acoustic emission RMS features with flank_wear_vb_mm across experiment series. Saves significant correlation via POST /findings with statistical_method, r_value, p_value, and conclusion for structured export.
target_output.data_model: MongoDB cutting_experiments: experiment_id UUID, tool_id string, tool_grade string, workpiece_material string, cutting_params nested (feed_rate_mmrev float, depth_of_cut_mm float, spindle_speed_rpm int, coolant string enum dry/flood/mql), start_ts datetime, end_ts datetime, status string enum running/complete/aborted, notes string. MongoDB sensor_channels: experiment_id ref, channel_type string enum spindle_load/acoustic_emission/feed_force/chip_image, sample_rate_hz int, record_count int, duration_s float, file_path string, quality_flag string enum ok/clipped/noisy/missing, captured_at datetime. MongoDB tool_wear_measurements: experiment_id ref, measurement_number int, flank_wear_vb_mm float, crater_wear_kt_mm float, measured_at datetime, image_path string. MongoDB correlation_findings: finding_id UUID, experiment_ids array of strings, hypothesis string, statistical_method string, r_value float, p_value float, conclusion string, exported_at datetime. Filesystem layout: /data/sensor-files/{experiment_id}/{channel_type}.npy and /data/chip-images/{experiment_id}/{measurement_number}.jpg.
target_output.api_design: All at /api/v1. POST /experiments body tool_id workpiece_material cutting_params returns experiment_id. GET /experiments query params material tool_id date_from date_to returns list of experiment summaries. GET /experiments/:id returns full experiment doc with sensor channel manifest. POST /sensor-data/:experiment_id/:channel_type accepts chunked numpy binary max 200MB returns channel_id and record_count. GET /sensor-data/:experiment_id/:channel_type/stream returns streaming .npy binary for SensorLoader. POST /wear-measurements/:experiment_id body flank_wear_vb_mm crater_wear_kt_mm returns measurement_id. GET /wear-measurements/:experiment_id returns list of wear measurements. POST /findings body experiment_ids hypothesis statistical_method r_value p_value conclusion returns finding_id. GET /findings returns structured JSON export list.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Custom JWT HS256 issued by POST /auth/token for API write access. GET read endpoints accessible within lab network without token for notebook researcher convenience. MongoDB 7 auth enabled; FastAPI uses dedicated MongoDB user with readWrite on lab_cutting_db only; admin credentials stored in .env file on lab server not in repository. HTTPS via self-signed certificate on lab NGINX reverse proxy. /data/sensor-files volume permissions: fastapi-api container runs as non-root with write access; jupyterlab container has read-only mount for direct numpy loading. No external network access from any service container.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: docker-compose.yml services: api FastAPI image built from Dockerfile volume-mounts /data/sensor-files and /data/chip-images with env_file .env; mongodb mongo:7 image with auth enabled and /data/mongodb persistent volume and MONGO_INITDB scripts creating lab user; jupyterlab scipy-notebook:latest with lab_client pip-installed and /data/sensor-files read-only mount; prometheus; grafana; node-exporter. GitLab CI MR gate runs pytest with mongomock fixtures covering all API routes, ruff lint, mypy strict. Main merge triggers SSH to lab server for docker-compose pull api and docker-compose up -d --no-deps api. Data volume backup: rsync /data to lab NAS nightly via cron. MongoDB dump to /data/backups nightly via mongodump cron container.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics via prometheus-fastapi-instrumentator: http_requests_total by route and status, sensor_ingest_bytes_total by channel_type, experiment_ingest_duration_seconds p50/p95. Prometheus node_exporter: disk usage alert when /data volume available bytes fall below 20% of total capacity triggering Grafana notification to lab Slack channel. Grafana dashboard: experiment ingestion rate per day, sensor file storage growth trend in GB, API error rate gauge, disk usage percentage. FastAPI structured JSON logs via structlog to stdout captured by docker logging driver.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero cloud cost; runs on existing lab server. Storage planning: 10kHz acoustic emission plus 1kHz spindle load for 5-minute cuts produces approximately 25MB of .npy files per experiment. At 50 experiments per month that is 1.2GB per month and 15GB per year, well within lab server 2TB disk. MongoDB metadata is negligible. When disk usage reaches 80% of allocated space the Prometheus alert triggers researcher review of archival strategy for oldest datasets.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP weeks 1-3: FastAPI scaffolding, MongoDB cutting_experiments and sensor_channels collections, sensor ingest with .npy file storage at /data/sensor-files, experiment CRUD endpoints, tool wear measurement endpoints, JWT auth, docker-compose with all services, GitLab CI with pytest and mongomock. Phase 2 weeks 4-6: lab_client package with ExperimentClient SensorLoader WearLoader; correlation analysis notebook templates; findings endpoint; JupyterLab container with lab_client pre-installed and notebook templates mounted. Phase 3 weeks 7-10: Feed force sensor adapter integration, chip morphology JPEG upload and storage, image-to-MongoDB metadata linking, enhanced notebook for multi-sensor feature extraction including RMS kurtosis and spectral entropy via scipy.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with type annotations throughout; mypy strict mode in CI. Pydantic v2 models for all API request and response schemas. pytest fixtures use mongomock for MongoDB unit tests; no real MongoDB instance required in CI pipeline. ruff replaces flake8 and isort. lab_client package must expose entirely synchronous API surface with no async or await visible to notebook users; all Motor async internals wrapped with asyncio.run. Notebook templates include assert statements checking experiment status before loading sensor data.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: .npy files and MongoDB sensor_channels metadata becoming out of sync if API crashes after file write but before MongoDB insert. Mitigation: SensorIngestService deletes .npy file and returns 500 if MongoDB insert fails; nightly cleanup cron removes orphaned .npy files with no corresponding sensor_channels record. Risk: Lab server disk filling unexpectedly from large experiments. Mitigation: Prometheus node_exporter alert at 80% disk usage with immediate Grafana notification. Tradeoff: numpy .npy file format over HDF5 trades per-experiment channel grouping for simpler ingest code and direct numpy.load compatibility without h5py dependency - acceptable at this prototype scale.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Sensor timeseries storage resolved as numpy .npy files on filesystem with MongoDB sensor_channels metadata reference to eliminate 16MB document limit. Notebook access pattern resolved as synchronous lab_client package pre-installed in JupyterLab container. Disk monitoring resolved via Prometheus node_exporter with 80% threshold Grafana alert. Feed force and chip image integration deferred to Phase 3.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision fixing MongoDB 16MB timeseries storage and adding lab_client helper for researcher Jupyter notebooks in CNC tool wear correlation prototype.

profile.domain: Medical Device Quality Management - CAPA Desktop Application

project_goal.value: Digitize the deviation and CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action) management process for a medical device manufacturer, replacing paper-based forms with a desktop application supporting deviation logging, root cause analysis workflow, corrective action tracking, and effectiveness verification.
project_goal.rationale: Paper CAPA process creates audit risk and loses traceability required by FDA 21 CFR Part 820

target_users.value: Quality engineers, quality managers, and production supervisors at a medical device manufacturing facility
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified in QMS process mapping workshop

project_class.rationale: Deployed to managed workstations on facility network; Electron provides cross-platform Windows compatibility

capabilities.rationale: Electron shell, FastAPI backend service, PostgreSQL persistence, Azure AD auth, ERP notification integration

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-stage approval workflow with mandatory reviewer signatures and scheduled effectiveness checks

risk_level.rationale: FDA 21 CFR Part 820 and ISO 13485 regulate CAPA processes for medical devices; non-compliance can trigger FDA warning letters

data_sensitivity.rationale: CAPA records contain nonconformance details that are confidential and subject to FDA audit

external_exposure.rationale: Application runs on facility intranet; no internet-facing endpoints

access_model.value: Azure AD with RBAC roles: QualityEngineer, QualityManager, ProductionSupervisor, ReadOnly
access_model.rationale: FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requires user authentication and audit trail for electronic signatures

feature_scope.rationale: Covers full CAPA lifecycle per ISO 13485 clause 8.5.2

mvp_scope.rationale: Preventive action and advanced analytics can follow after initial deployment and user validation

frontend_stack.rationale: Electron provides desktop packaging for Windows workstations; React for component reuse with existing web team

backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI provides async performance for concurrent API requests; runs as NSSM Windows service alongside Electron

privacy_retention_policy.rationale: FDA 21 CFR Part 820.180 requires QSR records retained at least 2 years beyond device lifetime, typically 15 years

future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP roadmap pending IQ/OQ/PQ validation completion

constraints.rationale: FDA guidance on computer system validation requires documented installation, operational, and performance qualification

observability_baseline.rationale: Facility IT standard monitoring stack

execution_preference.rationale: Computer system validation requirements mandate formal test execution and sign-off before production deployment

compliance_context.value: FDA 21 CFR Part 820 quality system regulation for medical devices, ISO 13485:2016 CAPA requirements clause 8.5.2, FDA 21 CFR Part 11 electronic records and electronic signatures, GAMP 5 computer system validation guidance
compliance_context.rationale: Risk level high and data sensitivity confidential trigger compliance requirement

project.goal: Digitize deviation and CAPA lifecycle management for FDA-regulated medical device manufacturer
project.target_users: Quality engineers, quality managers, production supervisors

frontend.state: Zustand for workflow state, React Query for API calls to local FastAPI

backend.framework: FastAPI running as NSSM Windows service

security.auth: Azure AD MSAL with MFA
security.esig: FDA 21 CFR Part 11 electronic signature with meaning and date
security.audit: Immutable audit trail for all record field changes

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server via Private Endpoint
data.schema: deviations, capa_records, root_cause_analyses, corrective_actions, effectiveness_verifications, esignatures, audit_trail

devops.distribution: SCCM for Electron app, NSSM Windows service on app server

reasoner_reviews.product: Digitizing the CAPA process addresses a real compliance risk: paper-based CAPA records are frequently cited in FDA warning letters for inadequate traceability. The multi-stage approval workflow with electronic signatures is the core differentiator. MVP scoping to deviation logging and corrective action is appropriate given the CSV validation overhead.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Electron plus FastAPI Windows service is an unusual but workable architecture for a managed desktop application in a regulated environment. The key constraint is that the FastAPI service and PostgreSQL must be part of the validated system perimeter. The NSSM Windows service approach means updates must go through the same SCCM change control as the Electron app.
reasoner_reviews.security: FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requires that electronic signatures include the printed name of the signer, date and time of signing, and meaning of the signature. The current plan does not specify how e-signature meaning is captured or how the signature record is linked to the specific CAPA record version at time of signing. This is a compliance gap.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The IQ/OQ/PQ validation timeline is aggressive at 24 weeks for a regulated system. The test scripts for OQ must be drafted during development, not after. Recommend establishing a Validation Master Plan document during sprint 1 that defines the CSV scope and acceptance criteria.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The plan assigns effectiveness_verification scheduling to a background job but does not specify what happens if the background job is not running when a verification due date passes. For a regulated system, missed verification deadlines are a CAPA non-closure finding. The scheduler must have a catch-up mechanism and the escalation workflow must be defined.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with domain modules: deviations, capa_workflow, root_cause, corrective_actions, effectiveness, esignatures, audit_trail. CAPAWorkflowService orchestrates state transitions and triggers approval notifications. EffectivenessSchedulerService runs on startup and checks for overdue verifications.
backend.api_patterns: REST with OpenAPI 3.0. Azure AD JWT validated via python-jose. All state-changing endpoints require e-signature payload (meaning, re-auth token). Audit trail written atomically with every record update using PostgreSQL transaction.
backend.business_modules: Deviation module: creates deviation record with lot number, product code, detected_by, description, and initial severity classification. CAPA workflow module: manages state machine (open -> root_cause_assigned -> action_planned -> implementation -> effectiveness_check -> closed). Electronic signature module: captures signer Azure AD identity, meaning text, timestamp, and SHA-256 hash of record at time of signing.
backend.failure_handling: Background effectiveness scheduler uses a persistent job queue in PostgreSQL (scheduled_tasks table) with status tracking; if the service restarts, incomplete tasks are reprocessed on startup. Missed verifications set capa_status to verification_overdue and notify QualityManager via email.

frontend.app_structure: Electron 31 shell with React 18 SPA. Main process handles NSSM service health check on startup. Renderer process communicates with local FastAPI via localhost HTTP. Feature pages colocated by CAPA lifecycle stage.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Dashboard (open deviations by severity, overdue CAPAs, pending approvals), Deviation Entry form, Root Cause Analysis editor with fishbone template, Corrective Action editor, Approval Queue with e-signature modal (meaning dropdown, Azure AD re-auth), Effectiveness Verification form, CAPA Report PDF preview.
frontend.state_management: Zustand stores for active deviation context and CAPA workflow state machine. React Query for all FastAPI API calls with automatic retry on 503 during service startup.
frontend.frontend_risks: Electron auto-updates must be disabled or pointed to internal update server to maintain validated system configuration.

security.auth_design: Azure AD MSAL for Electron main process handles OAuth2 token acquisition. Tokens passed in Authorization header to local FastAPI. FastAPI validates JWT signature against Azure AD JWKS endpoint.
security.authorization_model: RBAC enforced in FastAPI via dependency injection: QualityEngineer (create deviation, submit root cause, implement action), QualityManager (approve CAPAs, sign effectiveness verifications, manage assignments), ProductionSupervisor (read own area deviations, confirm implementation), ReadOnly (view all records).
security.audit_and_logging_controls: audit_trail table: id UUID PK, record_type, record_id, field_name, old_value, new_value, changed_by, change_timestamp, ip_address. Written in same PostgreSQL transaction as every INSERT and UPDATE. Append-only: no UPDATE or DELETE on audit_trail rows, enforced via PostgreSQL row-level trigger.
security.security_risks: FDA 21 CFR Part 11 e-signature gaps: current design does not capture signature meaning or link signature to specific record hash. Must be addressed before CSV validation.

data.entities: deviations (id UUID PK, deviation_number SERIAL unique, lot_number, product_code, detected_by UUID FK users, description, severity enum minor/major/critical, status, created_at). capa_records (id UUID PK, capa_number SERIAL unique, deviation_id FK, owner_id FK, status enum CAPA workflow states, target_closure_date, closed_at). root_cause_analyses (id UUID PK, capa_id FK, method enum five_why/fishbone/fault_tree, root_cause_description, assigned_to FK, completed_at). corrective_actions (id UUID PK, capa_id FK, action_description, assigned_to FK, due_date, completed_at, verification_due_date). effectiveness_verifications (id UUID PK, corrective_action_id FK, scheduled_date, performed_by FK, outcome enum effective/ineffective/inconclusive, evidence_notes, completed_at). esignatures (id UUID PK, record_type, record_id, signer_id FK, meaning text, signed_at, record_hash SHA256). audit_trail (id UUID PK, record_type, record_id, field_name, old_value, new_value, changed_by FK, change_timestamp).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server via Private Endpoint. Encryption at rest enabled. Azure AD auth for database connections from FastAPI service account. Separate read-only user for New Relic query monitoring.
data.retention_and_deletion: CAPA records and audit_trail retained 15 years per FDA QSR. PostgreSQL backups: daily snapshots to Azure Backup with 35-day retention. No hard delete of any CAPA record; soft delete with status=archived for superseded records only.

devops.deployment_topology: FastAPI Windows service installed on facility app server via MSI; PostgreSQL on Azure Flexible Server accessed via Private Endpoint. Electron app packaged as NSIS installer and distributed via SCCM. Separate validated environment for IQ/OQ/PQ testing.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps Pipelines: PR gate - pytest 85% coverage, mypy, ruff, Playwright E2E for CAPA workflow happy path. Main merge builds Electron NSIS installer and FastAPI MSI, uploads to Azure Artifacts. Production deployment requires Change Control Board approval and updated validation documentation.
devops.devops_risks: Any code change post-validation requires re-execution of affected OQ test scripts and regulatory sign-off. Establish change impact assessment procedure in Validation Master Plan.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing an FDA 21 CFR Part 11 compliant CAPA desktop application for a medical device manufacturer. This is intentionally a flawed first-pass plan that omits the electronic signature meaning capture and record hash linkage required by 21 CFR Part 11, and underspecifies the effectiveness verification escalation workflow. These gaps are intentional to create a training example for auditor detection of compliance specification failures.
target_output.title: Medical Device CAPA Management Desktop Application
target_output.executive_summary: An Electron and FastAPI desktop application for quality engineers at a medical device manufacturer managing the full CAPA lifecycle from deviation logging through root cause analysis, corrective action approval, and effectiveness verification. Built on PostgreSQL 16 on Azure with Azure AD authentication, targeting FDA 21 CFR Part 820 and ISO 13485 compliance with computer system validation.
target_output.architecture_overview: Electron 31 shell with React 18 renderer communicates over localhost HTTP to a FastAPI Python service running as a Windows service on the facility app server. Azure AD MSAL handles OAuth2 token acquisition in the Electron main process. The FastAPI service connects to PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server via Private Endpoint with Azure AD service account authentication. All state-changing CAPA workflow operations require electronic signature capture. An audit_trail table records every field-level change in the same PostgreSQL transaction as the update. A background effectiveness scheduler checks for overdue verifications on service startup and daily via a scheduled task stored in PostgreSQL.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Electron 31, React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Zustand, React Query. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Alembic migrations. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server. Auth: Azure AD MSAL. Distribution: NSIS Electron installer via SCCM, FastAPI NSSM Windows service. CI/CD: Azure DevOps Pipelines. Observability: New Relic APM, structured JSON logging via structlog.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Deviation Entry: quality engineer logs deviation with deviation_number (auto-assigned), lot_number, product_code, description, initial severity classification (minor/major/critical). CAPA Initiation: QualityManager creates capa_record linked to deviation, assigns owner and target_closure_date. Root Cause Analysis: assigned engineer selects method (five-why, fishbone, fault-tree), documents root cause; QualityManager approves with e-signature. Corrective Action: engineer creates corrective_action with implementation plan and due date; QualityManager approves. Effectiveness Verification: system schedules verification 90 days post-closure; engineer completes verification form recording outcome (effective/ineffective/inconclusive); QualityManager signs off to close CAPA. CAPA Report: PDF generation showing full lifecycle timeline from deviation to closure.
target_output.system_components: Electron 31 main process (Azure AD MSAL, NSSM service health check, IPC bridge), React 18 renderer (CAPA workflow UI, e-signature modal, PDF preview), FastAPI Windows service (CAPA workflow state machine, audit trail writer, effectiveness scheduler, e-signature validator), PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server (deviations, capa_records, root_cause_analyses, corrective_actions, effectiveness_verifications, esignatures, audit_trail, scheduled_tasks), Azure AD tenant (user directory, RBAC group assignments, MFA policy), Azure Private Endpoint (network path from facility app server to PostgreSQL), New Relic APM (FastAPI performance monitoring).
target_output.workflows: CAPA Lifecycle: QualityEngineer creates deviation record with lot number and severity; system auto-assigns deviation_number. QualityManager initiates CAPA, assigns to owner. Engineer documents root cause using fishbone template and submits for approval. QualityManager reviews root cause analysis and approves with e-signature (e-signature modal captures meaning text from dropdown: 'Approved as documented'). Engineer creates corrective action with implementation steps and due date; QualityManager approves. Engineer marks implementation complete. System schedules effectiveness verification 90 days later in scheduled_tasks. Effectiveness Check: On scheduled date, system sends reminder notification to assigned engineer. Engineer opens effectiveness form, records outcome and evidence notes, submits. QualityManager reviews and signs e-signature to close CAPA record. If verification outcome is 'ineffective', CAPA status reverts to 'action_planned' and a new action is required.
target_output.data_model: deviations: id UUID PK, deviation_number SERIAL UNIQUE, lot_number TEXT, product_code TEXT, detected_by UUID FK, description TEXT, severity ENUM minor/major/critical, status ENUM open/capa_initiated/closed, detected_at TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ. capa_records: id UUID PK, capa_number SERIAL UNIQUE, deviation_id UUID FK, owner_id UUID FK, status ENUM open/root_cause_assigned/action_planned/in_implementation/effectiveness_check/verification_overdue/closed, target_closure_date DATE, closed_at TIMESTAMPTZ. root_cause_analyses: id UUID PK, capa_id UUID FK, method ENUM five_why/fishbone/fault_tree, root_cause_description TEXT, assigned_to UUID FK, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ. corrective_actions: id UUID PK, capa_id UUID FK, action_description TEXT, assigned_to UUID FK, due_date DATE, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, verification_due_date DATE. effectiveness_verifications: id UUID PK, corrective_action_id UUID FK, scheduled_date DATE, performed_by UUID FK, outcome ENUM effective/ineffective/inconclusive, evidence_notes TEXT, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ. esignatures: id UUID PK, record_type TEXT, record_id UUID, signer_id UUID FK, signed_at TIMESTAMPTZ. audit_trail: id UUID PK, record_type TEXT, record_id UUID, field_name TEXT, old_value TEXT, new_value TEXT, changed_by UUID FK, change_timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ.
target_output.api_design: REST at /api/v1. POST /deviations body lot_number product_code description severity returns deviation_id and deviation_number. GET /deviations query params status severity product_code returns paginated list. POST /capa-records body deviation_id owner_id target_closure_date returns capa_id. PATCH /capa-records/:id/root-cause body root_cause_description method esig_payload transitions to root_cause_assigned. PATCH /capa-records/:id/approve-root-cause body esig_payload QualityManager role required. POST /corrective-actions body capa_id action_description due_date. PATCH /corrective-actions/:id/complete body esig_payload. POST /effectiveness-verifications body corrective_action_id outcome evidence_notes. PATCH /effectiveness-verifications/:id/approve body esig_payload closes CAPA. GET /capa-records/:id/report generates PDF. GET /audit-trail/:record_type/:record_id returns change history.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure AD MSAL with MFA for all user roles. RBAC enforced in FastAPI via dependency injection checking Azure AD group membership. TLS 1.3 for all client-to-service communication. Audit trail written append-only in same PostgreSQL transaction as every record change; PostgreSQL row-level trigger prevents UPDATE and DELETE on audit_trail rows. Electronic signatures capture signer Azure AD identity and timestamp. FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requires signature meaning text - captured via dropdown (options: 'Approved as documented', 'Reviewed and accepted', 'Implemented as described'). Record hash linkage at time of signature is not implemented in this version and represents a known compliance gap requiring resolution before IQ/OQ/PQ. 15-year record retention enforced via Azure Backup policy.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: FastAPI packaged as MSI installer configuring NSSM Windows service on facility app server. Electron packaged as NSIS installer distributed via SCCM to quality department workstations. Azure DevOps Pipelines: PR gate runs pytest 85% coverage, mypy, ruff, Playwright E2E tests against dev database. Build pipeline produces versioned MSI and NSIS artifacts uploaded to Azure Artifacts. Production deployment requires Change Control Board approval, updated Validation Master Plan, and execution of affected OQ test scripts. Three environments: dev (Azure Flexible Server dev tier), validation (identical to production for IQ/OQ/PQ execution), production. Alembic database migrations applied during MSI install via pre-install script.
target_output.observability: New Relic APM agent on FastAPI service: transaction traces for CAPA workflow state transitions, error rate dashboard, CAPA approval latency p95. Structured JSON logging via structlog to Windows Event Log and log file. Custom metrics: open_deviations_by_severity gauge, overdue_effectiveness_verifications count, capa_cycle_time_days histogram. Alert: overdue_effectiveness_verifications > 0 triggers email to QualityManager and logs to Windows Event Log as Warning level for IT monitoring.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Azure Flexible Server Burstable B2ms tier: approximately $120/month. Azure Backup: approximately $30/month for 15-year retention policy with daily snapshots. Azure Private Endpoint: approximately $10/month. Total infrastructure approximately $160/month. Scales to 500 concurrent CAPA records comfortably on Burstable tier; upgrade to General Purpose for larger facilities with 2000+ active records.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 weeks 1-8: FastAPI service with deviation and CAPA record modules, PostgreSQL schema with Alembic migrations, Azure AD auth, audit trail, e-signature capture without record hash (known gap), Electron shell with React UI for deviation entry and CAPA workflow, Azure DevOps CI/CD, IQ documentation. Phase 2 weeks 9-16: Root cause analysis module with fishbone template, corrective action module, effectiveness verification scheduler, CAPA report PDF generation, OQ test script execution with formal sign-off. Phase 3 weeks 17-24: E-signature record hash linkage (closes 21 CFR Part 11 gap), effectiveness escalation workflow, PQ execution and CSV sign-off, SCCM distribution to production workstations.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 strict typing with mypy. SQLAlchemy 2.0 async session management with explicit transaction boundaries for audit trail writes. All CAPA workflow state transitions tested via pytest with state machine assertion helpers. Electron renderer TypeScript strict mode. Each release requires updated traceability matrix linking requirements to test cases for CSV. No production deployment without Change Control Board sign-off.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: e-signature record hash linkage missing in Phase 1 creates a known 21 CFR Part 11 gap that must be resolved before PQ sign-off. This is an intentional phasing decision documented in the Validation Master Plan. Risk: effectiveness verification scheduler missed deadlines if Windows service is stopped for maintenance. Mitigation: scheduler catch-up on service restart processes all overdue scheduled_tasks; escalation email sent for any task more than 24 hours overdue. Tradeoff: NSSM Windows service over Docker for facility IT compatibility with existing Windows server infrastructure - avoids Docker licensing cost and IT retraining. Tradeoff: PostgreSQL on Azure over on-premises SQL Server to leverage managed backup and high availability without facility DBA overhead.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Electronic signature record hash: deferred to Phase 3 with documented gap in Validation Master Plan. Effectiveness scheduler catch-up: processes all overdue scheduled_tasks on service restart. SCCM packaging: NSIS installer for Electron, MSI for FastAPI service, both signed with facility code signing certificate.

metadata.notes: Intentionally flawed plan: FDA 21 CFR Part 11 e-signature record hash linkage missing and acknowledged as gap; effectiveness verification escalation workflow incomplete. Trains auditor detection of compliance specification failures.

project_goal.value: Scalable REST API gateway normalizing telemetry from heterogeneous CNC, robotics, and PLC equipment into a unified JSON schema for upstream analytics and MES applications
project_goal.rationale: Core connectivity abstraction enabling multi-vendor equipment integration

target_users.value: Internal analytics platforms, MES applications, and upstream data consumers via REST
target_users.rationale: Service-to-service consumers, no human end users

project_class.rationale: Pure API backend with no UI, serving downstream application consumers

capabilities.value: backend, data, auth, public_api, integrations, realtime
capabilities.rationale: Protocol normalization requires integration and realtime streaming capabilities

complexity_level.rationale: Thousands of concurrent machine connections with high-frequency telemetry ingestion

risk_level.rationale: Internal-only exposure with sensitive operational data but no PCI or health data

data_sensitivity.rationale: Machine telemetry is proprietary operational data not classified as personal or financial

external_exposure.rationale: API consumed only by internal platform services behind VPC boundary

access_model.value: JWT bearer tokens per consumer application, scoped read/write permissions per machine asset group
access_model.rationale: Service-to-service auth with fine-grained asset scope control

feature_scope.value: Machine asset registration, protocol adapter configuration, telemetry normalization, SSE streaming, consumer rate limiting, connection health monitoring
feature_scope.rationale: Full connectivity gateway feature set

mvp_scope.value: Modbus and OPC-UA adapters, unified JSON schema emission, InfluxDB persistence, JWT auth, SSE streaming endpoint, basic rate limiting
mvp_scope.rationale: Core protocols covering majority of plant equipment

security_baseline.value: JWT RS256 signed tokens, TLS 1.3 in transit, API rate limiting per consumer, input schema validation on all ingestion paths
security_baseline.rationale: Standard internal service security posture

frontend_stack.value: none - API service only
frontend_stack.rationale: No UI component, pure REST and SSE service

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin framework
backend_stack.rationale: Go goroutine model ideal for high-concurrency protocol connection management

data_platform.value: InfluxDB for telemetry time-series, Redis 7 for rate limit counters and connection state cache
data_platform.rationale: InfluxDB optimized for high-frequency numeric tag writes, Redis for low-latency state lookups

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate with Application Load Balancer
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers with auto-scaling for variable telemetry load

privacy_retention_policy.value: Telemetry data retained 90 days in InfluxDB hot tier, archived to S3 Parquet for 2 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Operational data retention aligned with plant audit requirements

future_scope.value: EtherNet/IP and PROFINET protocol adapters, bidirectional write-back commands, webhook push delivery to consumers
future_scope.rationale: Protocol expansion and command dispatch planned for v2

constraints.value: Must handle 2,000 concurrent machine connections, p99 normalization latency under 500ms, deploy within existing AWS VPC
constraints.rationale: Plant connectivity SLA requirements

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM, custom metrics for messages/sec per adapter type, connection health dashboard, PagerDuty for adapter failure alerts
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate monitoring standard with gateway-specific visibility

execution_preference.value: API-first development with OpenAPI 3.1 contract before implementation
execution_preference.rationale: Downstream consumer teams need stable API contract for integration development

project.goal: Industrial machine connectivity gateway normalizing heterogeneous protocol telemetry to unified JSON
project.target_users: Internal MES, analytics, and data platform services

backend.framework: Gin with custom middleware for rate limiting and JWT validation
backend.api: RESTful with SSE streaming and OpenAPI 3.1

security.auth: JWT RS256 with per-consumer scopes
security.rate_limiting: Token bucket per consumer JWT subject

data.cache: Redis 7 for rate limit counters and connection state

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with Go test and race detector

confirmed_decisions.schema: unified JSON with timestamp, machine_id, tag_name, value, quality_code fields

reasoner_reviews.product: Gateway design correctly decouples upstream consumers from protocol-specific device quirks. The unified schema approach with quality_code field handles partial connectivity gracefully. Rate limiting per consumer prevents noisy-neighbor scenarios when analytics bursts reads.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go goroutines map well to maintaining thousands of persistent protocol connections. However, prior rounds flagged missing backpressure between the ingestion goroutines and InfluxDB writes — need explicit write buffer with overflow shedding to prevent OOM under burst load. Schema mapping configuration should be hot-reloadable to avoid restarts when new machine tags are added.
reasoner_reviews.security: JWT RS256 with asymmetric signing is appropriate for service-to-service. Consumer scope validation must happen at the Gin middleware layer before any handler executes. Connection-level TLS verification should be enforced at the ALB listener, not delegated to application code.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 2,000 concurrent machine connections is achievable with Go goroutines but requires tuning of Gin's server read/write timeouts and InfluxDB batch write intervals. Redis pipeline mode should be used for rate limit counter increments to reduce per-request latency.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Previous plan missed specifying schema versioning strategy — consumers will break if the unified JSON schema evolves. Need a schema_version field in every emitted message and an explicit deprecation policy for old schema versions.

backend.service_design: Protocol adapter registry pattern: each adapter (ModbusAdapter, OPCUAAdapter) implements a common Adapter interface with Connect(), Subscribe(), and Normalize() methods. Adapter instances managed by a connection pool supervisor goroutine that monitors health and reconnects on failure.
backend.api_patterns: GET /v1/machines (list registered assets), POST /v1/machines (register new asset with adapter config), GET /v1/machines/:id/stream (SSE endpoint for real-time tag updates), GET /v1/machines/:id/telemetry (cursor-paginated historical query), PATCH /v1/machines/:id/adapter-config (hot-reload adapter parameters without restart)
backend.failure_handling: Write buffer channel between normalization goroutines and InfluxDB batch writer with configurable depth (default 10,000 messages); when buffer exceeds 80% capacity, oldest messages are dropped and a backpressure_drops_total counter incremented. Circuit breaker on InfluxDB writes trips after 5 consecutive failures.
backend.scaling_notes: Horizontal scaling via ECS task count increase; sticky routing not required as SSE connections can re-establish to any task. Redis cluster for shared rate limit state across tasks.

security.auth_design: RS256 JWT validation middleware applied to all routes; public key fetched from internal JWKS endpoint at startup and rotated every 24 hours. Consumer subject extracted from JWT claims for rate limit key derivation.
security.authorization_model: Scope-based access: read:telemetry scope required for GET endpoints, write:machines scope for asset registration and config updates. Scopes embedded in JWT claims, validated per-route.
security.abuse_prevention: Token bucket rate limiter in Redis: 1,000 requests/min per consumer JWT subject for REST endpoints, separate SSE connection limit of 10 concurrent streams per consumer.

data.entities: machine_assets (machine_id, asset_name, protocol_type ENUM modbus/opc_ua/ethernet_ip, adapter_config_json, status ENUM online/offline/degraded, registered_at), telemetry_streams (measurement=machine_telemetry, tags: machine_id tag_name quality_code, fields: value, timestamp), schema_mappings (mapping_id, machine_id, source_address, target_tag_name, unit, scale_factor, offset), rate_limit_policies (consumer_subject, requests_per_min, max_sse_connections, updated_at), connection_health_records (machine_id, last_seen_at, consecutive_failures, adapter_type)
data.storage_design: InfluxDB measurement 'machine_telemetry' partitioned by machine_id tag; retention policy 90 days with continuous query downsampling to 1-minute resolution for long-term S3 archive. Redis HASH for per-machine connection state, ZSET for SSE subscriber tracking per machine.
data.data_risks: InfluxDB cardinality explosion if tag_name values include free-text device descriptions; enforce tag_name allowlist validation at normalization layer.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate service with 3-6 tasks behind ALB target group; ALB listener enforces TLS 1.3. Task definition mounts secrets from AWS Secrets Manager (InfluxDB token, Redis password, JWKS endpoint URL). Auto-scaling on custom metric: active_machine_connections > 1,500 per task triggers scale-out.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go vet, staticcheck, go test -race, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with 50% minimum healthy. Integration tests run against LocalStack (S3) and containerized InfluxDB in CI.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM agent in sidecar; custom metrics emitted via StatsD: messages_normalized_total by adapter_type, backpressure_drops_total, active_connections_gauge, sse_subscribers_gauge. Datadog dashboard for gateway throughput and adapter health.
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS deployment circuit breaker enabled; rolls back to previous task definition revision if health checks fail during 5-minute stabilization window.

ISS-001.title: Missing write backpressure on InfluxDB ingestion path
ISS-001.detail: Normalization goroutines write directly to InfluxDB batch writer with no buffering; under burst load from 2,000 machines this causes goroutine pile-up and OOM crashes in staging load tests.

history.note: First identified during architecture review
Plan acknowledged issue but implementation detail not specified

ISS-002.title: No schema versioning for unified telemetry JSON
ISS-002.detail: Unified JSON schema has no version field; schema evolution will break consumer integrations silently. Need schema_version in emitted messages and explicit consumer migration path.

history.note: Raised by critic reviewer in round 2
